title: AP Biology Unit 6 Question Bank v2

style_notes.easy_questions: definition-focused vocabulary checks with less application
style_notes.hard_questions: relevant distractors with minimal recycling, including partially correct College Board-style traps
style_notes.analysis_questions: clear, concrete experimental scenarios with understandable setups and interpretable data
style_notes.answer_keys: specific explanations for incorrect choices that identify the exact misconception or why the option is tempting but wrong
style_notes.carryover_policy: older valid question patterns may be kept if they still meet the updated quality standards

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'semiconservative replication'?
choices: the enzyme that relieves twisting and strain ahead of the replication fork
the enzyme that synthesizes short RNA primers during DNA replication
the DNA replication model in which each daughter DNA molecule contains one original strand and one newly synthesized strand
a short RNA segment that provides a starting point for DNA polymerase
questions.explain: Semiconservative replication is defined as the DNA replication model in which each daughter DNA molecule contains one original strand and one newly synthesized strand.
choice_explanations: This choice defines topoisomerase, not semiconservative replication. It is a real Unit 6 term, which makes it tempting, but it does not match the vocabulary word being asked about.
This choice defines primase, not semiconservative replication. It is a real Unit 6 term, which makes it tempting, but it does not match the vocabulary word being asked about.
Correct. Semiconservative replication is defined as the DNA replication model in which each daughter DNA molecule contains one original strand and one newly synthesized strand.
This choice defines RNA primer, not semiconservative replication. It is a real Unit 6 term, which makes it tempting, but it does not match the vocabulary word being asked about.

questions.text:
A DNA template strand for part of a gene is shown below.
3′-TAC GGA TTT CCG-5′
Which mRNA sequence would be transcribed from this template?
choices: AUG CCU AAA GGC
UAC GGA UUU CCG
CGG AAA UCC GUA
AUG CCU AAA GGA
questions.explain: The correct mRNA is 5′-AUG CCU AAA GGC-3′ because RNA is synthesized complementary to the 3′→5′ template strand.
choice_explanations: Correct. RNA polymerase reads the 3′→5′ DNA template and synthesizes a complementary RNA strand 5′→3′, with U in place of T.
This choice copies the template strand directly instead of making a complementary RNA sequence. That mistake treats the template as though it were the coding strand.
This choice reverses the nucleotide order. The transcript must be synthesized 5′→3′ while remaining complementary to the template, not written backward.
This choice is close to the correct transcript but changes one base, so it does not match the actual base-pairing rules for the given template.

questions.text: An mRNA contains the codon 5′-AUG-3′. Which tRNA anticodon would pair with that codon during translation?
choices: UAC
UAC
AUG
CAU
questions.explain: The anticodon is UAC because tRNA base-pairs complementarily with the codon on mRNA.
choice_explanations: This choice could pair with a different codon, but it is not complementary to the codon shown in the question.
Correct. The anticodon on tRNA must be complementary to the mRNA codon.
This choice repeats the codon itself. The anticodon is not identical to the codon; it base-pairs with it.
This choice has the right bases but in the reverse order. Anticodon matching depends on complementary pairing, not just having the same letters backward.

questions.text: During translation, one codon in an mRNA changes from GAA to GAG. Which type of mutation is this change most likely to represent?
choices: missense mutation
nonsense mutation
frameshift mutation
silent mutation
questions.explain: The codon changes from GAA to GAG, but both codons specify Glu, so the amino acid sequence does not change.
choice_explanations: A missense mutation changes one amino acid to another. That does not fit this codon change.
A nonsense mutation creates a stop codon. That only fits if the new codon is UAA, UAG, or UGA.
A frameshift requires an insertion or deletion that shifts the reading frame. This question changes one codon to another without adding or removing bases.
Correct. The codon changes from GAA to GAG, but both codons specify Glu, so the amino acid sequence does not change.

A missense mutation changes one amino acid to another. That does not fit this codon change.

questions.text: During translation, one codon in an mRNA changes from GAG to GAA. Which type of mutation is this change most likely to represent?
choices: frameshift mutation
silent mutation
missense mutation
nonsense mutation
questions.explain: The codon changes from GAG to GAA, but both codons specify Glu, so the amino acid sequence does not change.
choice_explanations: A frameshift requires an insertion or deletion that shifts the reading frame. This question changes one codon to another without adding or removing bases.
Correct. The codon changes from GAG to GAA, but both codons specify Glu, so the amino acid sequence does not change.
A missense mutation changes one amino acid to another. That does not fit this codon change.

questions.text: A portion of an mRNA originally reads 5′-AUG GCU UUU CCA-3′. After mutation, it reads 5′-AUG GCG UUU CCA-3′. Which statement best describes the likely effect of this change?
choices: Translation cannot begin because any substitution removes the start codon.
The mutation must be silent because only one nucleotide changed.
Only one codon is altered directly, so the reading frame remains the same.
Every codon after the mutation is likely to change because the reading frame shifts.
questions.explain: Only one codon is altered directly, so the reading frame remains the same.
choice_explanations: A substitution changes a base, but it does not automatically remove the start codon. That only happens if the changed codon specifically includes the AUG start site.
Some substitutions are silent, but others are missense or nonsense. A one-base change is not automatically silent.
Correct. A substitution changes one base without shifting the reading frame, so only one codon is directly changed.
This describes a frameshift, which is much more typical of single-base insertion or deletion, not substitution.

questions.text: A portion of an mRNA originally reads 5′-AUG GCU UUU CCA-3′. After mutation, it reads 5′-AUG GCU AUU UCC A-3′. Which statement best describes the likely effect of this change?
choices: This change is best described as a silent substitution.
Only one codon is altered directly, so the reading frame remains the same.
Many downstream codons are likely to change because the reading frame shifts.
The mutation must have no effect if the protein is still translated.
questions.explain: Many downstream codons are likely to change because the reading frame shifts.
choice_explanations: This is not a substitution at all; bases were added or removed, which changes the reading frame.
A single-base insertion or deletion does not leave the frame unchanged. It usually shifts how all downstream triplets are read.
Correct. A one-base insertion or deletion usually causes a frameshift, changing many codons after the mutation site.
A protein may still be translated after a frameshift, but its amino acid sequence can be dramatically altered.

questions.text: A mutation occurs in the promoter sequence of a gene. Which outcome is most likely?
choices: It would automatically create a frameshift in every transcript produced by the cell.
decrease transcription because RNA polymerase binds less efficiently
It would only matter if the mutation changed an amino acid in the protein, because noncoding DNA has no role in gene expression.
It would stop DNA replication across the entire chromosome, because any gene mutation prevents copying of the DNA.
questions.explain: decrease transcription because RNA polymerase binds less efficiently
choice_explanations: Frameshifts occur when nucleotides are inserted or deleted in coding sequence not in multiples of three. A mutation in the site described does not automatically cause that outcome.
Correct. Because promoters are binding sites for transcription machinery, a mutation there can reduce how often transcription starts.
This is a classic trap. Many noncoding regions are regulatory, and mutations there can strongly affect gene expression without changing a protein's amino acid sequence.
A mutation in one gene or regulatory region does not automatically stop chromosome-wide DNA replication. Replication and gene expression are different processes.

questions.text: A mutation occurs in an intron splice site in a eukaryotic gene. Which outcome is most likely?

It would only matter if the mutation changed an amino acid in the protein, because noncoding DNA has no role in gene expression.
It would stop DNA replication across the entire chromosome, because any gene mutation prevents copying of the DNA.
It would automatically create a frameshift in every transcript produced by the cell.

questions.text: In a cell, helicase is inhibited. Which result is most likely?
choices: Transcription will be the only process affected, because DNA replication and transcription use entirely different DNA regions and enzymes.
Ribosomes will stop binding to mRNA, because all replication proteins work directly in translation.
The number of chromosomes in the cell will instantly double, because replication errors always increase chromosome count.
the two parental DNA strands will not separate efficiently at the replication fork
questions.explain: the two parental DNA strands will not separate efficiently at the replication fork
choice_explanations: This answer confuses replication with transcription. The condition described directly interferes with DNA replication, even if later gene-expression effects are possible.
This choice mixes replication proteins with translation. Ribosomes bind mRNA during translation, not DNA replication proteins.
Replication problems can affect DNA synthesis fidelity or completion, but they do not instantly double chromosome number.
Correct. Helicase breaks the hydrogen bonds that hold the strands together.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why DNA polymerase can add nucleotides only to a 3′ end?
choices: both strands must be synthesized 3′→5′ so the polymerase can move toward the fork on each strand
the lagging strand is produced because DNA polymerase cannot read an antiparallel template
one new strand is synthesized continuously while the other is synthesized in fragments
the strand with more primers must be the leading strand, because continuous synthesis requires repeated starts
questions.explain: one new strand is synthesized continuously while the other is synthesized in fragments
choice_explanations: DNA polymerase synthesizes new nucleic acid only 5′→3′, not 3′→5′. That rule is exactly why leading and lagging strands differ.
DNA polymerase can read either template strand as long as it adds nucleotides 5′→3′ to the new strand. The issue is synthesis direction, not inability to read the template.
Correct. Because the parental strands are antiparallel, the same polymerase rule creates leading and lagging strand synthesis.
This is backward. Repeated priming is needed for the lagging strand because it is synthesized in multiple fragments, not for the leading strand.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why the two DNA template strands run antiparallel?
choices: the lagging strand is produced because DNA polymerase cannot read an antiparallel template
the lagging strand must be made discontinuously
the strand with more primers must be the leading strand, because continuous synthesis requires repeated starts
both strands must be synthesized 3′→5′ so the polymerase can move toward the fork on each strand
questions.explain: the lagging strand must be made discontinuously
choice_explanations: DNA polymerase can read either template strand as long as it adds nucleotides 5′→3′ to the new strand. The issue is synthesis direction, not inability to read the template.
Correct. Antiparallel templates plus 5′→3′ synthesis create the need for Okazaki fragments.
This is backward. Repeated priming is needed for the lagging strand because it is synthesized in multiple fragments, not for the leading strand.
DNA polymerase synthesizes new nucleic acid only 5′→3′, not 3′→5′. That rule is exactly why leading and lagging strands differ.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why a replication fork is moving to the right?
choices: the template running 3′→5′ toward the fork is used to make the leading strand
the strand with more primers must be the leading strand, because continuous synthesis requires repeated starts
both strands must be synthesized 3′→5′ so the polymerase can move toward the fork on each strand
the lagging strand is produced because DNA polymerase cannot read an antiparallel template

This is backward. Repeated priming is needed for the lagging strand because it is synthesized in multiple fragments, not for the leading strand.

DNA polymerase can read either template strand as long as it adds nucleotides 5′→3′ to the new strand. The issue is synthesis direction, not inability to read the template.

choice_explanations: This is backward. Repeated priming is needed for the lagging strand because it is synthesized in multiple fragments, not for the leading strand.
DNA polymerase synthesizes new nucleic acid only 5′→3′, not 3′→5′. That rule is exactly why leading and lagging strands differ.

This is backward. Repeated priming is needed for the lagging strand because it is synthesized in multiple fragments, not for the leading strand.
DNA polymerase synthesizes new nucleic acid only 5′→3′, not 3′→5′. That rule is exactly why leading and lagging strands differ.
DNA polymerase can read either template strand as long as it adds nucleotides 5′→3′ to the new strand. The issue is synthesis direction, not inability to read the template.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why both strands are replicated at the same overall time?
choices: the strand with more primers must be the leading strand, because continuous synthesis requires repeated starts
both strands must be synthesized 3′→5′ so the polymerase can move toward the fork on each strand
the lagging strand is produced because DNA polymerase cannot read an antiparallel template
this does not mean both are synthesized in the same physical manner
questions.explain: this does not mean both are synthesized in the same physical manner
choice_explanations: This is backward. Repeated priming is needed for the lagging strand because it is synthesized in multiple fragments, not for the leading strand.
DNA polymerase synthesizes new nucleic acid only 5′→3′, not 3′→5′. That rule is exactly why leading and lagging strands differ.
DNA polymerase can read either template strand as long as it adds nucleotides 5′→3′ to the new strand. The issue is synthesis direction, not inability to read the template.
Correct. Leading and lagging strands are both replicated during S phase, but one is continuous and the other discontinuous.

questions.text: In a eukaryotic cell, a promoter is mutated so RNA polymerase binds less efficiently. Which consequence is most likely?
choices: The gene will be translated directly from DNA, so mature mRNA processing will not matter
less mRNA is likely to be produced from that gene
DNA replication of the entire chromosome will stop immediately, because all transcription defects directly block replication forks
The amino acid sequence must stay unchanged, because transcription-related changes affect only DNA and never RNA or protein output
questions.explain: less mRNA is likely to be produced from that gene
choice_explanations: Ribosomes translate mRNA, not DNA. In eukaryotes, mature mRNA processing can be essential before translation occurs.
Correct. Promoter strength affects transcription initiation frequency.
This exaggerates the effect and confuses transcription with DNA replication. The prompt is about RNA production or processing, not direct fork collapse across the chromosome.
Transcription and RNA processing can strongly change how much protein is made or what sequence is translated, even without changing the DNA coding region itself.

questions.text: A biologist observes that histone acetylation near a promoter increases. Which interpretation is most accurate?
choices: transcription of the nearby gene is likely to increase because the chromatin becomes more accessible
the DNA base sequence in that region must have changed, because gene expression cannot change unless the gene itself mutates
RNA polymerase will bind more strongly because acetylation or methylation directly changes the charge of DNA itself
all genes in the genome will be turned on or off to the same degree, because epigenetic regulation affects every chromosome equally
questions.explain: transcription of the nearby gene is likely to increase because the chromatin becomes more accessible
choice_explanations: Correct. Acetylation generally loosens histone-DNA interactions and opens chromatin.
This distractor ignores regulation without sequence change. Epigenetic and regulatory changes can alter expression while the nucleotide sequence stays the same.

This is a partially correct trap. Acetylation and methylation do affect gene expression, but histone acetylation modifies histone tails rather than DNA itself, and the outcome depends on chromatin accessibility, not a simple 'polymerase sticks harder because DNA charge changed' explanation.
Gene regulation is usually gene-specific or region-specific. A change at one promoter, enhancer, or chromatin region does not automatically affect every gene equally.

questions.text: A biologist observes that DNA methylation increases in a promoter region. Which interpretation is most accurate?
choices: the DNA base sequence in that region must have changed, because gene expression cannot change unless the gene itself mutates

all genes in the genome will be turned on or off to the same degree, because epigenetic regulation affects every chromosome equally
transcription is likely to decrease because the promoter becomes less accessible to transcription machinery
questions.explain: transcription is likely to decrease because the promoter becomes less accessible to transcription machinery
choice_explanations: This distractor ignores regulation without sequence change. Epigenetic and regulatory changes can alter expression while the nucleotide sequence stays the same.

Gene regulation is usually gene-specific or region-specific. A change at one promoter, enhancer, or chromatin region does not automatically affect every gene equally.
Correct. Promoter methylation is commonly associated with reduced gene expression.

genes in that region are generally less likely to be transcribed
the DNA base sequence in that region must have changed, because gene expression cannot change unless the gene itself mutates

Acetylation and methylation do affect gene expression, but histone acetylation modifies histone tails rather than DNA itself, and the outcome depends on chromatin accessibility, not a simple 'polymerase sticks harder because DNA charge changed' explanation.
Gene regulation is usually gene-specific or region-specific. A change at one promoter, enhancer, or chromatin region does not automatically affect every gene equally.
Correct. Heterochromatin is tightly packed and less accessible.
This distractor ignores regulation without sequence change. Epigenetic and regulatory changes can alter expression while the nucleotide sequence stays the same.

questions.text: In a bacterial culture, lactose is present and glucose is present. Which statement best predicts transcription of the lac operon?
choices: The lac operon will be transcribed only at a low level because lactose removes the repressor, but glucose keeps cAMP low.
The lac operon will be strongly transcribed whenever glucose is absent, even if lactose is also absent, because CAP activation completely overrides the repressor.
The lac operon will be off only when both lactose and glucose are present, because bacteria never regulate two sugars at once.
The lac operon will be highly expressed whenever lactose is absent, because the cell must produce enzymes to search for lactose.
questions.explain: The lac operon will be transcribed only at a low level because lactose removes the repressor, but glucose keeps cAMP low.
choice_explanations: Correct. The lac operon responds to both lactose and glucose, so the final output reflects repressor control plus catabolite repression.
This is partly right about CAP-cAMP helping when glucose is low, but it is still wrong overall. If lactose is absent, the repressor remains bound and CAP does not fully override that repression.
This ignores the actual logic of the lac operon. The operon is usually low or off when lactose is absent, and it is highest when lactose is present and glucose is absent.
This reverses the operon's purpose. When lactose is absent, the cell usually avoids making lactose-metabolizing enzymes.

This reverses the operon's purpose. When lactose is absent, the cell usually avoids making lactose-metabolizing enzymes.
Correct. The lac operon responds to both lactose and glucose, so the final output reflects repressor control plus catabolite repression.

questions.text: A bacterial cell is grown in medium with a high concentration of tryptophan. Which statement best predicts expression of the trp operon?
choices: The trp operon behaves like an inducible operon, so transcription is highest when tryptophan is abundant and binds the operator directly.
The trp operon is always on because anabolic pathways must run constantly regardless of metabolite levels.
Tryptophan activates RNA polymerase directly, so the operon is transcribed more when tryptophan concentration rises.
The trp operon will be repressed because tryptophan acts as a corepressor that activates the repressor.
questions.explain: The trp operon will be repressed because tryptophan acts as a corepressor that activates the repressor.
choice_explanations: This is a tempting comparison to the lac operon, but it is wrong. The trp operon is repressible, not inducible, and tryptophan helps activate the repressor rather than serving as an inducer.
This ignores feedback regulation. Biosynthetic operons are often reduced when the end product is already plentiful.
Tryptophan does not activate RNA polymerase directly in the trp operon. It acts as a corepressor by helping the repressor bind DNA.
Correct. The trp operon is a repressible operon that is generally more active when tryptophan is low and less active when tryptophan is abundant.

questions.text: A bacterial cell is grown in medium with a low concentration of tryptophan. Which statement best predicts expression of the trp operon?
choices: The trp operon is always on because anabolic pathways must run constantly regardless of metabolite levels.
Tryptophan activates RNA polymerase directly, so the operon is transcribed more when tryptophan concentration rises.
The trp operon will be transcribed because the repressor is inactive when tryptophan is scarce.
The trp operon behaves like an inducible operon, so transcription is highest when tryptophan is abundant and binds the operator directly.
questions.explain: The trp operon will be transcribed because the repressor is inactive when tryptophan is scarce.
choice_explanations: This ignores feedback regulation. Biosynthetic operons are often reduced when the end product is already plentiful.
Tryptophan does not activate RNA polymerase directly in the trp operon. It acts as a corepressor by helping the repressor bind DNA.
Correct. The trp operon is a repressible operon that is generally more active when tryptophan is low and less active when tryptophan is abundant.
This is a tempting comparison to the lac operon, but it is wrong. The trp operon is repressible, not inducible, and tryptophan helps activate the repressor rather than serving as an inducer.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why a stem cell differentiates into a muscle cell?
choices: all genes should be expressed equally once a cell becomes specialized, because specialization requires maximum protein production
genes for muscle-specific proteins are likely to be expressed at higher levels in that cell
the cell must have lost all genes unrelated to its current function, because differentiation works by deleting unneeded DNA
the DNA sequence of every gene must have changed, because specialization requires new versions of genes
questions.explain: genes for muscle-specific proteins are likely to be expressed at higher levels in that cell
choice_explanations: Specialized cells usually express a selective subset of genes, not every gene at equal levels.
Correct. Differentiation changes which genes are turned on, not the genome each somatic cell inherited.
Most differentiated cells keep nearly the same genome. They specialize mainly by regulating which genes are expressed, not by deleting all unused genes.
Specialization usually does not require rewriting the whole genome. It depends largely on regulatory control of existing genes.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why a mutation disrupts a homeobox gene?
choices: the expression pattern of other developmental genes may change
the cell must have lost all genes unrelated to its current function, because differentiation works by deleting unneeded DNA
the DNA sequence of every gene must have changed, because specialization requires new versions of genes
all genes should be expressed equally once a cell becomes specialized, because specialization requires maximum protein production
questions.explain: the expression pattern of other developmental genes may change
choice_explanations: Correct. Homeobox genes encode regulators that control developmental pathways.
Most differentiated cells keep nearly the same genome. They specialize mainly by regulating which genes are expressed, not by deleting all unused genes.
Specialization usually does not require rewriting the whole genome. It depends largely on regulatory control of existing genes.
Specialized cells usually express a selective subset of genes, not every gene at equal levels.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why two cell types from the same organism have different shapes and functions?
choices: the cell must have lost all genes unrelated to its current function, because differentiation works by deleting unneeded DNA
the DNA sequence of every gene must have changed, because specialization requires new versions of genes
all genes should be expressed equally once a cell becomes specialized, because specialization requires maximum protein production
they most likely differ in which genes are expressed, not in the entire DNA sequence they contain
questions.explain: they most likely differ in which genes are expressed, not in the entire DNA sequence they contain
choice_explanations: Most differentiated cells keep nearly the same genome. They specialize mainly by regulating which genes are expressed, not by deleting all unused genes.
Specialization usually does not require rewriting the whole genome. It depends largely on regulatory control of existing genes.
Specialized cells usually express a selective subset of genes, not every gene at equal levels.
Correct. Somatic cells usually share the same genome but use different parts of it.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why a neuron and a liver cell are compared?
choices: the DNA sequence of every gene must have changed, because specialization requires new versions of genes
all genes should be expressed equally once a cell becomes specialized, because specialization requires maximum protein production
they are expected to contain the same genes but different sets of active genes
the cell must have lost all genes unrelated to its current function, because differentiation works by deleting unneeded DNA
questions.explain: they are expected to contain the same genes but different sets of active genes
choice_explanations: Specialization usually does not require rewriting the whole genome. It depends largely on regulatory control of existing genes.
Specialized cells usually express a selective subset of genes, not every gene at equal levels.
Correct. Specialized cells differ mainly by gene expression patterns.
Most differentiated cells keep nearly the same genome. They specialize mainly by regulating which genes are expressed, not by deleting all unused genes.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why a signal peptide is deleted from a secreted protein?
choices: all genes should be expressed equally once a cell becomes specialized, because specialization requires maximum protein production
the protein may still be translated but fail to enter the correct transport pathway
the cell must have lost all genes unrelated to its current function, because differentiation works by deleting unneeded DNA
the DNA sequence of every gene must have changed, because specialization requires new versions of genes
questions.explain: the protein may still be translated but fail to enter the correct transport pathway

Correct. Signal peptides direct proteins to destinations such as the ER.

questions.text: A researcher wants to amplify a short specific DNA region. Which method or tool is most appropriate?
choices: gel electrophoresis
DNA ligase
ribosome
PCR
questions.explain: PCR
choice_explanations: Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments after they already exist; it does not selectively amplify a target sequence.
DNA ligase joins DNA fragments together but does not by itself generate repeated copies of a chosen segment.
Ribosomes translate mRNA into protein, which is a completely different process.
Correct. PCR uses primers and cycles of heating/cooling to make many copies of a chosen DNA sequence.

questions.text: A researcher wants to separate DNA fragments by size. Which method or tool is most appropriate?
choices: restriction enzyme
reverse transcriptase
gel electrophoresis
PCR
questions.explain: gel electrophoresis
choice_explanations: Restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific sequences, but the question asks about separating fragments after they exist.
Reverse transcriptase makes DNA from RNA; it does not sort fragments by length.
Correct. DNA fragments move through a gel, with smaller fragments usually traveling farther.
PCR amplifies DNA but does not separate preexisting fragments by size on a gel.

questions.text: A researcher wants to cut DNA at a particular recognition sequence. Which method or tool is most appropriate?
choices: RNA polymerase
restriction enzyme
DNA ligase
PCR
questions.explain: restriction enzyme
choice_explanations: RNA polymerase synthesizes RNA from DNA during transcription, not targeted DNA cutting.
Correct. Restriction enzymes recognize and cleave specific DNA sequences.
DNA ligase seals DNA fragments together rather than cutting at a recognition site.
PCR amplifies DNA between primers; it does not recognize and cleave a specific sequence.

questions.text: A researcher wants to make DNA from an mRNA template. Which method or tool is most appropriate?
choices: reverse transcriptase
DNA polymerase
restriction enzyme
RNA polymerase
questions.explain: reverse transcriptase
choice_explanations: Correct. Reverse transcriptase synthesizes DNA using RNA as the template.
DNA polymerase synthesizes DNA from a DNA template, not from RNA.
Restriction enzymes cut DNA; they do not synthesize a complementary DNA strand from RNA.
RNA polymerase makes RNA from DNA, which is the opposite direction from the one described.

questions.text: A researcher wants to insert foreign DNA into bacteria using a circular DNA carrier. Which method or tool is most appropriate?
choices: ribosome
spliceosome
DNA methyltransferase
plasmid vector
questions.explain: plasmid vector
choice_explanations: Ribosomes synthesize proteins; they are not DNA carriers used to introduce genes into bacteria.
Spliceosomes process eukaryotic pre-mRNA and are unrelated to carrying foreign DNA into bacteria.
DNA methyltransferase modifies DNA chemically but is not a cloning carrier.
Correct. Plasmids are commonly used as cloning vectors in bacteria.

questions.text: A researcher wants to measure whether a promoter is active in living cells. Which method or tool is most appropriate?
choices: Okazaki fragment
operator
reporter gene
restriction enzyme
questions.explain: reporter gene
choice_explanations: An Okazaki fragment is a replication intermediate on the lagging strand, not an expression readout.
An operator is a regulatory DNA site; by itself it does not provide an easily measurable output.
Correct. A reporter gene provides an easily detectable readout of gene expression.
Restriction enzymes cut DNA and do not directly report whether transcription is occurring.

questions.text: A researcher wants to edit a specific genomic sequence with a guide RNA. Which method or tool is most appropriate?
choices: promoter
CRISPR-Cas9
DNA ligase
reverse transcriptase
questions.explain: CRISPR-Cas9
choice_explanations: A promoter is a DNA sequence involved in transcription initiation, not a gene-editing tool.
Correct. CRISPR-Cas9 targets a DNA sequence using guide-RNA complementarity.
DNA ligase can join DNA ends but does not target a genomic sequence using a guide RNA.
Reverse transcriptase copies RNA into DNA and is not a programmable DNA-cutting system.

questions.text: A researcher wants to create DNA that combines sequences from two sources. Which method or tool is most appropriate?
choices: recombinant DNA
chromatin
mRNA splicing
telomerase
questions.explain: recombinant DNA
choice_explanations: Correct. Recombinant DNA joins genetic material from different origins.
Chromatin is the DNA-protein complex in eukaryotic cells, not the specific term for DNA assembled from different sources.
mRNA splicing removes introns from RNA and is unrelated to combining DNA from different origins.
Telomerase extends chromosome ends and does not create combined DNA molecules from separate sources.

DNA ligase

Ribosomes translate mRNA into protein, which is a completely different process.
Correct. Primer placement determines which region is amplified.

questions.text: A researcher wants to make a DNA library from processed mRNA. Which method or tool is most appropriate?
choices: restriction digestion
DNA methylation
cDNA synthesis
PCR
questions.explain: cDNA synthesis
choice_explanations: Restriction digestion cuts DNA and does not convert expressed mRNA information into DNA form.
DNA methylation modifies DNA chemically and is not used to create a DNA library from mature transcripts.
Correct. cDNA reflects expressed genes because it is synthesized from mature mRNA.
PCR amplifies DNA, but cDNA specifically refers to DNA synthesized from an mRNA template.

questions.text: A researcher observes that a microRNA binds the 3′ end of an mRNA. Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: translation will increase because any RNA-binding event helps ribosomes attach more effectively
the effect will apply equally to every gene in the genome, because regulatory proteins do not act on specific targets
protein production from that mRNA often decreases because translation is blocked or the mRNA is degraded
the DNA sequence of the encoded protein must have changed, because regulation after transcription cannot affect protein output
questions.explain: protein production from that mRNA often decreases because translation is blocked or the mRNA is degraded
choice_explanations: This is a partially correct-sounding claim because some RNA-binding proteins can increase translation, but the situation described does not automatically help ribosomes. Many binding events actually block translation or destabilize the RNA.
Regulatory effects are usually specific to genes or mRNAs that contain the relevant binding sites.
Correct. miRNA acts after transcription by targeting specific mRNAs.
Protein output can change at transcriptional or post-transcriptional levels without altering the coding DNA sequence.

questions.text: A researcher observes that an enhancer is moved far from a gene in a construct but still present. Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: the effect will apply equally to every gene in the genome, because regulatory proteins do not act on specific targets
the enhancer may still affect transcription because enhancers can act at a distance
the DNA sequence of the encoded protein must have changed, because regulation after transcription cannot affect protein output
translation will increase because any RNA-binding event helps ribosomes attach more effectively
questions.explain: the enhancer may still affect transcription because enhancers can act at a distance
choice_explanations: Regulatory effects are usually specific to genes or mRNAs that contain the relevant binding sites.
Correct. Enhancers do not need to sit immediately next to the promoter to function.
Protein output can change at transcriptional or post-transcriptional levels without altering the coding DNA sequence.

questions.text: A researcher observes that a promoter is intact but an activator-binding site is deleted. Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: basal transcription may remain, but high-level activated transcription may drop
the DNA sequence of the encoded protein must have changed, because regulation after transcription cannot affect protein output
translation will increase because any RNA-binding event helps ribosomes attach more effectively
the effect will apply equally to every gene in the genome, because regulatory proteins do not act on specific targets

choice_explanations: Correct. Promoters and enhancers have different regulatory roles.
Protein output can change at transcriptional or post-transcriptional levels without altering the coding DNA sequence.

the effect will apply equally to every gene in the genome, because regulatory proteins do not act on specific targets
transcription of the operon may increase even without changing the coding genes
questions.explain: transcription of the operon may increase even without changing the coding genes
choice_explanations: Protein output can change at transcriptional or post-transcriptional levels without altering the coding DNA sequence.

Regulatory effects are usually specific to genes or mRNAs that contain the relevant binding sites.
Correct. Operator mutations can change regulation without altering enzyme sequences.

questions.explain: less protein may be translated even if mRNA amount stays high
choice_explanations: This is a partially correct-sounding claim because some RNA-binding proteins can increase translation, but the situation described does not automatically help ribosomes. Many binding events actually block translation or destabilize the RNA.
Regulatory effects are usually specific to genes or mRNAs that contain the relevant binding sites.
Correct. Translation can be regulated after transcription.
Protein output can change at transcriptional or post-transcriptional levels without altering the coding DNA sequence.

questions.text: A researcher observes that an mRNA has a longer poly-A tail after hormone treatment. Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: the effect will apply equally to every gene in the genome, because regulatory proteins do not act on specific targets
the mRNA may become more stable and yield more protein
the DNA sequence of the encoded protein must have changed, because regulation after transcription cannot affect protein output
translation will increase because any RNA-binding event helps ribosomes attach more effectively
questions.explain: the mRNA may become more stable and yield more protein
choice_explanations: Regulatory effects are usually specific to genes or mRNAs that contain the relevant binding sites.
Correct. Poly-A tail length can influence mRNA stability and translation efficiency.
Protein output can change at transcriptional or post-transcriptional levels without altering the coding DNA sequence.

This is a partially correct-sounding claim because some RNA-binding proteins can increase translation, but the situation described does not automatically help ribosomes. Many binding events actually block translation or destabilize the RNA.

questions.text: A researcher observes that a cell receives a signal that activates a transcription factor. Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: expression of genes with that factor's binding sites may increase
the DNA sequence of the encoded protein must have changed, because regulation after transcription cannot affect protein output
translation will increase because any RNA-binding event helps ribosomes attach more effectively
the effect will apply equally to every gene in the genome, because regulatory proteins do not act on specific targets
questions.explain: expression of genes with that factor's binding sites may increase
choice_explanations: Correct. Transcription factors regulate specific target genes.
Protein output can change at transcriptional or post-transcriptional levels without altering the coding DNA sequence.

This is a partially correct-sounding claim because some RNA-binding proteins can increase translation, but the situation described does not automatically help ribosomes. Many binding events actually block translation or destabilize the RNA.
Regulatory effects are usually specific to genes or mRNAs that contain the relevant binding sites.

questions.text: A researcher observes that a mutation deletes a silencer. Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: the DNA sequence of the encoded protein must have changed, because regulation after transcription cannot affect protein output
translation will increase because any RNA-binding event helps ribosomes attach more effectively
the effect will apply equally to every gene in the genome, because regulatory proteins do not act on specific targets
transcription of that gene may rise because a negative regulatory element is gone
questions.explain: transcription of that gene may rise because a negative regulatory element is gone
choice_explanations: Protein output can change at transcriptional or post-transcriptional levels without altering the coding DNA sequence.

This is a partially correct-sounding claim because some RNA-binding proteins can increase translation, but the situation described does not automatically help ribosomes. Many binding events actually block translation or destabilize the RNA.
Regulatory effects are usually specific to genes or mRNAs that contain the relevant binding sites.
Correct. Silencers reduce transcription when regulatory proteins bind.

questions.text: A researcher observes that a proto-oncogene is overexpressed. Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: translation will increase because any RNA-binding event helps ribosomes attach more effectively
the effect will apply equally to every gene in the genome, because regulatory proteins do not act on specific targets
cell division signaling may become excessive even if the protein's sequence is unchanged
the DNA sequence of the encoded protein must have changed, because regulation after transcription cannot affect protein output
questions.explain: cell division signaling may become excessive even if the protein's sequence is unchanged
choice_explanations: This is a partially correct-sounding claim because some RNA-binding proteins can increase translation, but the situation described does not automatically help ribosomes. Many binding events actually block translation or destabilize the RNA.
Regulatory effects are usually specific to genes or mRNAs that contain the relevant binding sites.
Correct. Too much normal growth-promoting protein can still drive abnormal proliferation.
Protein output can change at transcriptional or post-transcriptional levels without altering the coding DNA sequence.

questions.text: A researcher observes that a tumor suppressor gene is still transcribed, but the protein cannot bind DNA. Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: the effect will apply equally to every gene in the genome, because regulatory proteins do not act on specific targets
regulation may fail because protein function, not just transcript presence, matters
the DNA sequence of the encoded protein must have changed, because regulation after transcription cannot affect protein output
translation will increase because any RNA-binding event helps ribosomes attach more effectively
questions.explain: regulation may fail because protein function, not just transcript presence, matters
choice_explanations: Regulatory effects are usually specific to genes or mRNAs that contain the relevant binding sites.
Correct. A normal mRNA amount does not guarantee a working regulatory protein.
Protein output can change at transcriptional or post-transcriptional levels without altering the coding DNA sequence.

This is a partially correct-sounding claim because some RNA-binding proteins can increase translation, but the situation described does not automatically help ribosomes. Many binding events actually block translation or destabilize the RNA.

questions.text: A student sets up a PCR reaction but accidentally leaves out one of the two primers. Which statement best predicts the result?
choices: PCR requires primers that flank the target region, so removing one primer prevents normal amplification of that specific segment.
PCR can proceed normally without primers because DNA polymerase can always start a new strand on its own.
Gel electrophoresis will replace the missing primer because both techniques work on DNA fragments.
The missing primer will only affect translation, not DNA amplification, because primers are used by ribosomes.
questions.explain: PCR requires primers that flank the target region, so removing one primer prevents normal amplification of that specific segment.
choice_explanations: Correct. PCR depends on primers to provide starting points for DNA polymerase at both ends of the region being amplified.
DNA polymerase cannot begin synthesis from nothing; it extends from an existing 3′ end provided by a primer.
Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments after amplification; it does not substitute for a primer during PCR.
This confuses DNA amplification with translation. Primers are used in PCR and replication-related processes, not by ribosomes.

questions.text:
Wild-type bacteria are grown in four media conditions. β-galactosidase activity is measured as follows:
Condition 1: +lactose, −glucose → 95 units
Condition 2: +lactose, +glucose → 28 units
Condition 3: −lactose, −glucose → 4 units
Condition 4: −lactose, +glucose → 3 units
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The operon requires lactose for derepression, and glucose lowers expression through catabolite repression.
The data show that lactose directly activates RNA polymerase by binding the promoter, so repressor and CAP proteins are irrelevant.
The operon is controlled only by glucose, because any condition with glucose present should fully block transcription regardless of lactose or mutations.
The results must be caused by a mutation in the lacZ coding region, because regulatory DNA cannot change expression patterns this strongly.
questions.explain: The operon requires lactose for derepression, and glucose lowers expression through catabolite repression.
choice_explanations: Correct. Lactose presence produces the biggest increase, while glucose mainly lowers expression when lactose is present.
This is a partially correct-sounding trap because lactose does affect lac operon expression, but it does so by binding the repressor, not by binding RNA polymerase at the promoter.
Glucose matters through CAP-cAMP, but lactose control and regulatory mutations still matter. The data clearly show more than one level of regulation.
Regulatory sequences and regulatory proteins can dramatically change transcription without altering the lacZ coding sequence itself.

questions.text:
A mutant strain has a repressor that cannot bind the operator. β-galactosidase activity is:
+lactose, −glucose → 98 units
+lactose, +glucose → 35 units
−lactose, −glucose → 92 units
−lactose, +glucose → 30 units
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The data show that lactose directly activates RNA polymerase by binding the promoter, so repressor and CAP proteins are irrelevant.
The operon is controlled only by glucose, because any condition with glucose present should fully block transcription regardless of lactose or mutations.
The results must be caused by a mutation in the lacZ coding region, because regulatory DNA cannot change expression patterns this strongly.
The operator is no longer repressed by LacI, so lactose is no longer required for transcription, but glucose still reduces expression through CAP-cAMP.
questions.explain: The operator is no longer repressed by LacI, so lactose is no longer required for transcription, but glucose still reduces expression through CAP-cAMP.
choice_explanations: This is a partially correct-sounding trap because lactose does affect lac operon expression, but it does so by binding the repressor, not by binding RNA polymerase at the promoter.
Glucose matters through CAP-cAMP, but lactose control and regulatory mutations still matter. The data clearly show more than one level of regulation.
Regulatory sequences and regulatory proteins can dramatically change transcription without altering the lacZ coding sequence itself.
Correct. High expression even without lactose points to failed repression, while the glucose effect remains.

questions.text:
A strain has a mutation in the CAP binding site upstream of the lac operon. β-galactosidase activity is:
+lactose, −glucose → 22 units
+lactose, +glucose → 18 units
−lactose, −glucose → 2 units
−lactose, +glucose → 2 units
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The operon is controlled only by glucose, because any condition with glucose present should fully block transcription regardless of lactose or mutations.
The results must be caused by a mutation in the lacZ coding region, because regulatory DNA cannot change expression patterns this strongly.
Loss of CAP activation prevents strong transcription when glucose is absent, even though lactose still removes the repressor.
The data show that lactose directly activates RNA polymerase by binding the promoter, so repressor and CAP proteins are irrelevant.
questions.explain: Loss of CAP activation prevents strong transcription when glucose is absent, even though lactose still removes the repressor.
choice_explanations: Glucose matters through CAP-cAMP, but lactose control and regulatory mutations still matter. The data clearly show more than one level of regulation.
Regulatory sequences and regulatory proteins can dramatically change transcription without altering the lacZ coding sequence itself.
Correct. The small difference between low and high glucose suggests the CAP site is not functioning.
This is a partially correct-sounding trap because lactose does affect lac operon expression, but it does so by binding the repressor, not by binding RNA polymerase at the promoter.

questions.text:
Wild-type cells are shifted from medium lacking lactose to medium containing lactose and no glucose. lacZ mRNA rises from 2 copies/cell to 80 copies/cell in 10 minutes.
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The results must be caused by a mutation in the lacZ coding region, because regulatory DNA cannot change expression patterns this strongly.
Adding lactose inactivates the repressor, allowing rapid transcription of the lac operon under conditions that also favor CAP activation.
The data show that lactose directly activates RNA polymerase by binding the promoter, so repressor and CAP proteins are irrelevant.
The operon is controlled only by glucose, because any condition with glucose present should fully block transcription regardless of lactose or mutations.
questions.explain: Adding lactose inactivates the repressor, allowing rapid transcription of the lac operon under conditions that also favor CAP activation.
choice_explanations: Regulatory sequences and regulatory proteins can dramatically change transcription without altering the lacZ coding sequence itself.
Correct. The fast rise in lacZ transcript after lactose addition indicates regulation at transcription initiation, not a slow DNA change.
This is a partially correct-sounding trap because lactose does affect lac operon expression, but it does so by binding the repressor, not by binding RNA polymerase at the promoter.
Glucose matters through CAP-cAMP, but lactose control and regulatory mutations still matter. The data clearly show more than one level of regulation.

questions.text:
A researcher adds glucose to a culture that already contains lactose. β-galactosidase activity drops from 90 units to 24 units within one generation, while lacI sequence remains unchanged.
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The decrease is best explained by catabolite repression rather than by a change in the repressor gene.
The data show that lactose directly activates RNA polymerase by binding the promoter, so repressor and CAP proteins are irrelevant.
The operon is controlled only by glucose, because any condition with glucose present should fully block transcription regardless of lactose or mutations.
The results must be caused by a mutation in the lacZ coding region, because regulatory DNA cannot change expression patterns this strongly.
questions.explain: The decrease is best explained by catabolite repression rather than by a change in the repressor gene.
choice_explanations: Correct. Glucose lowers cAMP, so CAP activation falls even though lactose may still keep the repressor inactive.
This is a partially correct-sounding trap because lactose does affect lac operon expression, but it does so by binding the repressor, not by binding RNA polymerase at the promoter.
Glucose matters through CAP-cAMP, but lactose control and regulatory mutations still matter. The data clearly show more than one level of regulation.
Regulatory sequences and regulatory proteins can dramatically change transcription without altering the lacZ coding sequence itself.

Correct. Glucose lowers cAMP, so CAP activation falls even though lactose may still keep the repressor inactive.
This is a partially correct-sounding trap because lactose does affect lac operon expression, but it does so by binding the repressor, not by binding RNA polymerase at the promoter.
Glucose matters through CAP-cAMP, but lactose control and regulatory mutations still matter. The data clearly show more than one level of regulation.

questions.text:
Wild-type bacteria are grown in medium with different tryptophan concentrations. trp operon mRNA levels are:
0 µM Trp → 100 units
10 µM Trp → 55 units
100 µM Trp → 6 units
Which conclusion is most supported?
choices: The data show that tryptophan is an inducer that turns the operon on directly at the promoter, like lactose in the lac operon.
The results must reflect slower DNA replication in high-tryptophan medium, because transcription of biosynthetic genes is not regulated by end products.
Because the trp operon codes for anabolic enzymes, it should be highest when tryptophan is abundant.
Tryptophan represses the operon because it helps activate the repressor, so expression falls as tryptophan becomes abundant.
questions.explain: Tryptophan represses the operon because it helps activate the repressor, so expression falls as tryptophan becomes abundant.
choice_explanations: This distractor borrows logic from the lac operon, but the trp operon is repressible, not inducible. Tryptophan helps turn it off, not on.
The pattern is about regulation of gene expression, not chromosome replication rate.
This reverses feedback inhibition logic. If tryptophan is already abundant, the cell usually reduces synthesis of it.
Correct. The inverse relationship between tryptophan concentration and mRNA is the expected pattern for a repressible operon.

questions.text:
Cultured liver cells are treated with a histone deacetylase inhibitor. After treatment, chromatin near Gene A becomes more accessible and Gene A mRNA rises from 1.0-fold to 6.5-fold relative to untreated cells.
Which explanation is best supported by the evidence?
choices: The results show that acetyl groups are added to DNA bases to make the sequence easier for ribosomes to read.
Because the DNA sequence stayed the same, gene expression should not have changed.
Blocking deacetylation likely increased histone acetylation, opening chromatin and increasing transcription of Gene A.
The increase in transcription must mean the promoter DNA acquired new coding information for RNA polymerase.
questions.explain: Blocking deacetylation likely increased histone acetylation, opening chromatin and increasing transcription of Gene A.
choice_explanations: This is the kind of near-miss College Board trap you asked for. Acetylation is involved in gene regulation, but histone acetylation modifies histone tails, not DNA bases, and ribosomes read mRNA, not promoter DNA.
Epigenetic regulation specifically allows gene expression to change without altering the base sequence.
Correct. The increased accessibility and higher mRNA point to transcriptional activation through chromatin remodeling.
Promoters regulate transcription through binding and accessibility; they do not need new coding information added to them for RNA polymerase to work.

questions.text:
Researchers methylate the promoter of Gene B in vitro before introducing the construct into cells. Reporter activity falls from 100 units to 12 units.
Which explanation is best supported by the evidence?
choices: Because the DNA sequence stayed the same, gene expression should not have changed.
Promoter methylation likely reduced transcription by making the regulatory region less accessible or less favorable for transcription-factor binding.
The increase in transcription must mean the promoter DNA acquired new coding information for RNA polymerase.
The results show that acetyl groups are added to DNA bases to make the sequence easier for ribosomes to read.
questions.explain: Promoter methylation likely reduced transcription by making the regulatory region less accessible or less favorable for transcription-factor binding.
choice_explanations: Epigenetic regulation specifically allows gene expression to change without altering the base sequence.
Correct. The change occurs at a promoter-level regulatory step, not because the coding sequence was altered.
Promoters regulate transcription through binding and accessibility; they do not need new coding information added to them for RNA polymerase to work.

This is the kind of near-miss College Board trap you asked for. Acetylation is involved in gene regulation, but histone acetylation modifies histone tails, not DNA bases, and ribosomes read mRNA, not promoter DNA.

questions.text:
Neural precursor cells differentiate into neurons. At Gene C, histone acetylation increases while DNA sequence remains unchanged. Gene C transcript levels rise markedly.
Which explanation is best supported by the evidence?
choices: The data support gene regulation during differentiation rather than mutation of Gene C.
The increase in transcription must mean the promoter DNA acquired new coding information for RNA polymerase.
The results show that acetyl groups are added to DNA bases to make the sequence easier for ribosomes to read.
Because the DNA sequence stayed the same, gene expression should not have changed.
questions.explain: The data support gene regulation during differentiation rather than mutation of Gene C.
choice_explanations: Correct. Differentiation changes expression programs without requiring different DNA sequences in each cell.
Promoters regulate transcription through binding and accessibility; they do not need new coding information added to them for RNA polymerase to work.

This is the kind of near-miss College Board trap you asked for. Acetylation is involved in gene regulation, but histone acetylation modifies histone tails, not DNA bases, and ribosomes read mRNA, not promoter DNA.
Epigenetic regulation specifically allows gene expression to change without altering the base sequence.

questions.text:
A chromatin immunoprecipitation assay shows that Gene D is enriched for heterochromatin marks in skin cells but not in muscle cells. Gene D mRNA is abundant in muscle and nearly absent in skin.
Which explanation is best supported by the evidence?
choices: The increase in transcription must mean the promoter DNA acquired new coding information for RNA polymerase.
The results show that acetyl groups are added to DNA bases to make the sequence easier for ribosomes to read.
Because the DNA sequence stayed the same, gene expression should not have changed.
The chromatin state is consistent with lower transcription of Gene D in skin cells.
questions.explain: The chromatin state is consistent with lower transcription of Gene D in skin cells.
choice_explanations: Promoters regulate transcription through binding and accessibility; they do not need new coding information added to them for RNA polymerase to work.

This is the kind of near-miss College Board trap you asked for. Acetylation is involved in gene regulation, but histone acetylation modifies histone tails, not DNA bases, and ribosomes read mRNA, not promoter DNA.
Epigenetic regulation specifically allows gene expression to change without altering the base sequence.
Correct. Heterochromatin is generally associated with reduced access by transcription machinery.

This is the kind of near-miss College Board trap you asked for. Acetylation is involved in gene regulation, but histone acetylation modifies histone tails, not DNA bases, and ribosomes read mRNA, not promoter DNA.
Epigenetic regulation specifically allows gene expression to change without altering the base sequence.
Correct. Differentiation changes expression programs without requiring different DNA sequences in each cell.

questions.text:
A chromatin immunoprecipitation assay shows that Gene D is enriched for heterochromatin marks in skin cells but not in muscle cells. Gene D mRNA is abundant in muscle and nearly absent in skin.
Which explanation is best supported by the evidence?
choices: The results show that acetyl groups are added to DNA bases to make the sequence easier for ribosomes to read.
Because the DNA sequence stayed the same, gene expression should not have changed.
The chromatin state is consistent with lower transcription of Gene D in skin cells.
The increase in transcription must mean the promoter DNA acquired new coding information for RNA polymerase.
questions.explain: The chromatin state is consistent with lower transcription of Gene D in skin cells.
choice_explanations: This is the kind of near-miss College Board trap you asked for. Acetylation is involved in gene regulation, but histone acetylation modifies histone tails, not DNA bases, and ribosomes read mRNA, not promoter DNA.
Epigenetic regulation specifically allows gene expression to change without altering the base sequence.
Correct. Heterochromatin is generally associated with reduced access by transcription machinery.
Promoters regulate transcription through binding and accessibility; they do not need new coding information added to them for RNA polymerase to work.

questions.text:
Cells treated with a demethylating drug show increased expression of a tumor suppressor gene that had been transcriptionally silent.
Which explanation is best supported by the evidence?
choices: Because the DNA sequence stayed the same, gene expression should not have changed.
Removing inhibitory methylation can restore expression of a silenced gene without changing the DNA sequence itself.
The increase in transcription must mean the promoter DNA acquired new coding information for RNA polymerase.
The results show that acetyl groups are added to DNA bases to make the sequence easier for ribosomes to read.
questions.explain: Removing inhibitory methylation can restore expression of a silenced gene without changing the DNA sequence itself.
choice_explanations: Epigenetic regulation specifically allows gene expression to change without altering the base sequence.
Correct. The result fits epigenetic regulation of a tumor suppressor gene.

questions.text:
Cultured liver cells are treated with a histone deacetylase inhibitor. After treatment, chromatin near Gene A becomes more accessible and Gene A mRNA rises from 1.0-fold to 6.5-fold relative to untreated cells.
Which explanation is best supported by the evidence?
choices: Blocking deacetylation likely increased histone acetylation, opening chromatin and increasing transcription of Gene A.
The increase in transcription must mean the promoter DNA acquired new coding information for RNA polymerase.
The results show that acetyl groups are added to DNA bases to make the sequence easier for ribosomes to read.
Because the DNA sequence stayed the same, gene expression should not have changed.
questions.explain: Blocking deacetylation likely increased histone acetylation, opening chromatin and increasing transcription of Gene A.
choice_explanations: Correct. The increased accessibility and higher mRNA point to transcriptional activation through chromatin remodeling.

Epigenetic regulation specifically allows gene expression to change without altering the base sequence.

questions.text:
Researchers methylate the promoter of Gene B in vitro before introducing the construct into cells. Reporter activity falls from 100 units to 12 units.
Which explanation is best supported by the evidence?
choices: The increase in transcription must mean the promoter DNA acquired new coding information for RNA polymerase.
The results show that acetyl groups are added to DNA bases to make the sequence easier for ribosomes to read.
Because the DNA sequence stayed the same, gene expression should not have changed.
Promoter methylation likely reduced transcription by making the regulatory region less accessible or less favorable for transcription-factor binding.

Correct. The change occurs at a promoter-level regulatory step, not because the coding sequence was altered.

questions.text:
Three GFP reporter constructs are tested in the same eukaryotic cells.
Construct 1: promoter + enhancer → 120 fluorescence units
Construct 2: promoter only → 18 units
Construct 3: enhancer only, no promoter → 2 units
Which interpretation best fits the experiment?
choices: The data prove that RNA polymerase binds directly to any enhancer and no promoter is necessary.
The enhancer increases transcription but still requires a promoter where transcription machinery can assemble.
The coding sequence of GFP or luciferase must be different among constructs, because regulatory DNA outside the protein-coding region cannot change expression this much.
Enhancers are translated into protein, so deleting one should reduce fluorescence by shortening the reporter protein.
questions.explain: The enhancer increases transcription but still requires a promoter where transcription machinery can assemble.
choice_explanations: Enhancers help regulate transcription, often through transcription factors, but they do not replace the promoter where core transcription machinery assembles.
Correct. High output with promoter+enhancer and almost none with enhancer alone shows that the enhancer does not replace the promoter.
Regulatory DNA can produce large expression differences without altering the reporter protein sequence itself.
Enhancers are DNA regulatory elements, not translated parts of the protein-coding region.

questions.text:
A mutation deletes the TATA-box region of a promoter. Luciferase reporter output falls from 95 units to 4 units.
Which interpretation best fits the experiment?
choices: The deleted promoter sequence is important for transcription initiation.
The coding sequence of GFP or luciferase must be different among constructs, because regulatory DNA outside the protein-coding region cannot change expression this much.
Enhancers are translated into protein, so deleting one should reduce fluorescence by shortening the reporter protein.
The data prove that RNA polymerase binds directly to any enhancer and no promoter is necessary.
questions.explain: The deleted promoter sequence is important for transcription initiation.
choice_explanations: Correct. Promoter mutations can strongly reduce reporter output even when the coding region of luciferase is unchanged.
Regulatory DNA can produce large expression differences without altering the reporter protein sequence itself.
Enhancers are DNA regulatory elements, not translated parts of the protein-coding region.
Enhancers help regulate transcription, often through transcription factors, but they do not replace the promoter where core transcription machinery assembles.

questions.text:
A transcription factor is added to cells expressing a reporter that contains its enhancer-binding site. Reporter activity rises from 10 units to 85 units.
Which interpretation best fits the experiment?
choices: The coding sequence of GFP or luciferase must be different among constructs, because regulatory DNA outside the protein-coding region cannot change expression this much.
Enhancers are translated into protein, so deleting one should reduce fluorescence by shortening the reporter protein.
The data prove that RNA polymerase binds directly to any enhancer and no promoter is necessary.
The transcription factor likely acts as an activator for that enhancer-containing construct.
questions.explain: The transcription factor likely acts as an activator for that enhancer-containing construct.
choice_explanations: Regulatory DNA can produce large expression differences without altering the reporter protein sequence itself.
Enhancers are DNA regulatory elements, not translated parts of the protein-coding region.
Enhancers help regulate transcription, often through transcription factors, but they do not replace the promoter where core transcription machinery assembles.
Correct. The effect depends on a regulatory interaction between the factor and its DNA binding site.

questions.text:
A silencer element is inserted upstream of a reporter gene. Reporter expression drops from 60 units to 8 units only when a specific repressor protein is also present.
Which interpretation best fits the experiment?
choices: Enhancers are translated into protein, so deleting one should reduce fluorescence by shortening the reporter protein.
The data prove that RNA polymerase binds directly to any enhancer and no promoter is necessary.
The silencer reduces transcription only when the matching regulatory protein can bind it.
The coding sequence of GFP or luciferase must be different among constructs, because regulatory DNA outside the protein-coding region cannot change expression this much.
questions.explain: The silencer reduces transcription only when the matching regulatory protein can bind it.
choice_explanations: Enhancers are DNA regulatory elements, not translated parts of the protein-coding region.
Enhancers help regulate transcription, often through transcription factors, but they do not replace the promoter where core transcription machinery assembles.
Correct. The DNA element itself is not enough; the corresponding repressor must also be present.
Regulatory DNA can produce large expression differences without altering the reporter protein sequence itself.

questions.text:

The coding sequence of GFP or luciferase must be different among constructs, because regulatory DNA outside the protein-coding region cannot change expression this much.
Enhancers are translated into protein, so deleting one should reduce fluorescence by shortening the reporter protein.

The silencer reduces transcription only when the matching regulatory protein can bind it.
questions.explain: The silencer reduces transcription only when the matching regulatory protein can bind it.
choice_explanations: Regulatory DNA can produce large expression differences without altering the reporter protein sequence itself.
Enhancers are DNA regulatory elements, not translated parts of the protein-coding region.
Enhancers help regulate transcription, often through transcription factors, but they do not replace the promoter where core transcription machinery assembles.
Correct. The DNA element itself is not enough; the corresponding repressor must also be present.

questions.text:
Two identical reporters differ only in enhancer orientation, but both produce similar expression levels.
Which interpretation best fits the experiment?
choices: Enhancers are translated into protein, so deleting one should reduce fluorescence by shortening the reporter protein.
The data prove that RNA polymerase binds directly to any enhancer and no promoter is necessary.
The enhancer can function in either orientation in this experiment, which is a common property of many enhancers.
The coding sequence of GFP or luciferase must be different among constructs, because regulatory DNA outside the protein-coding region cannot change expression this much.
questions.explain: The enhancer can function in either orientation in this experiment, which is a common property of many enhancers.
choice_explanations: Enhancers are DNA regulatory elements, not translated parts of the protein-coding region.
Enhancers help regulate transcription, often through transcription factors, but they do not replace the promoter where core transcription machinery assembles.
Correct. The data suggest the enhancer acts as a regulatory DNA element rather than as part of the coding sequence.
Regulatory DNA can produce large expression differences without altering the reporter protein sequence itself.

questions.text:
A 1200-bp DNA fragment is cut with a restriction enzyme that has one recognition site in the fragment. After digestion, the gel shows bands at 700 bp and 500 bp.
Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: The enzyme cut the original fragment one time, producing two smaller fragments whose sizes add to the original length.
The band that moved farthest contains the largest DNA fragments because larger molecules are pulled more strongly by the electric field.
The total length of the bands after digestion does not need to match the starting DNA because electrophoresis destroys some nucleotides during separation.
A different banding pattern must mean a completely different gene was loaded, because a single mutation cannot change a restriction digest pattern.
questions.explain: The enzyme cut the original fragment one time, producing two smaller fragments whose sizes add to the original length.
choice_explanations: Correct. Restriction digestion changes fragment number, and gel electrophoresis separates the resulting pieces by size.
This reverses how gel electrophoresis works. Smaller DNA fragments usually move farther through the gel matrix.
Electrophoresis separates DNA fragments; it does not destroy bases so that total length changes.
A single base change can create or destroy a restriction site and therefore alter the digestion pattern.

questions.text:
Plasmid P is 3000 bp. After digestion with enzyme X, a single band appears at 3000 bp. After digestion with enzymes X and Y together, bands appear at 1800 bp and 1200 bp.
Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: The band that moved farthest contains the largest DNA fragments because larger molecules are pulled more strongly by the electric field.
The total length of the bands after digestion does not need to match the starting DNA because electrophoresis destroys some nucleotides during separation.
A different banding pattern must mean a completely different gene was loaded, because a single mutation cannot change a restriction digest pattern.
Enzyme X likely does not cut this plasmid, while adding enzyme Y generates two fragments that sum to the plasmid length.
questions.explain: Enzyme X likely does not cut this plasmid, while adding enzyme Y generates two fragments that sum to the plasmid length.
choice_explanations: This reverses how gel electrophoresis works. Smaller DNA fragments usually move farther through the gel matrix.
Electrophoresis separates DNA fragments; it does not destroy bases so that total length changes.
A single base change can create or destroy a restriction site and therefore alter the digestion pattern.
Correct. A single undigested-sized band after one-enzyme treatment suggests that enzyme had no cut site in the plasmid.

Correct. Loss of a restriction site changes the banding pattern without requiring any change in gel conditions.
