questions.text:
A PCR reaction is run for 30 cycles with both primers, nucleotides, thermostable DNA polymerase, and template DNA. A strong band appears at 450 bp. In a parallel tube missing primers, no 450-bp band appears.
Which explanation best matches the outcome?
choices: PCR failed because ribosomes were not added; they are needed to read the primers and build DNA.
Denaturation is the step when primers extend the new DNA strand, so lowering annealing temperature should not affect which sequence is amplified.
The result proves that PCR changes the DNA sequence of the gene itself in the original organism.
Primers are required to define the target region and provide starting points for DNA polymerase during PCR.
questions.explain: Primers are required to define the target region and provide starting points for DNA polymerase during PCR.
choice_explanations: Ribosomes are used for translation, not DNA amplification in PCR.
This choice mixes up PCR stages. Denaturation separates DNA strands; annealing is the step where primers bind and specificity matters.
PCR amplifies DNA in a reaction tube. It does not change the DNA sequence in the original organism simply by being run.
Correct. Without primers, the polymerase cannot efficiently begin amplification of the chosen segment.

questions.text:
A student lowers the annealing temperature far below the recommended value. The gel shows several unexpected bands in addition to the target band.
Which explanation best matches the outcome?
choices: Denaturation is the step when primers extend the new DNA strand, so lowering annealing temperature should not affect which sequence is amplified.
The result proves that PCR changes the DNA sequence of the gene itself in the original organism.
The low annealing temperature likely allowed primers to bind nonspecifically, producing extra PCR products.
PCR failed because ribosomes were not added; they are needed to read the primers and build DNA.
questions.explain: The low annealing temperature likely allowed primers to bind nonspecifically, producing extra PCR products.
choice_explanations: This choice mixes up PCR stages. Denaturation separates DNA strands; annealing is the step where primers bind and specificity matters.
PCR amplifies DNA in a reaction tube. It does not change the DNA sequence in the original organism simply by being run.
Correct. Annealing temperature affects primer specificity.
Ribosomes are used for translation, not DNA amplification in PCR.

questions.text:
A PCR reaction is prepared without template DNA. After cycling, no product is detected.
Which explanation best matches the outcome?
choices: The result proves that PCR changes the DNA sequence of the gene itself in the original organism.
PCR cannot amplify a target sequence if no template containing that sequence is present.
PCR failed because ribosomes were not added; they are needed to read the primers and build DNA.
Denaturation is the step when primers extend the new DNA strand, so lowering annealing temperature should not affect which sequence is amplified.
questions.explain: PCR cannot amplify a target sequence if no template containing that sequence is present.
choice_explanations: PCR amplifies DNA in a reaction tube. It does not change the DNA sequence in the original organism simply by being run.
Correct. All of the enzymes and primers may be present, but there is nothing to copy.
Ribosomes are used for translation, not DNA amplification in PCR.
This choice mixes up PCR stages. Denaturation separates DNA strands; annealing is the step where primers bind and specificity matters.

questions.text:
A mutation occurs in one primer-binding site on the template. PCR yield drops sharply even though the target region itself is mostly unchanged.
Which explanation best matches the outcome?
choices: A mismatch at a primer-binding site can reduce amplification because primer annealing becomes less effective.
PCR failed because ribosomes were not added; they are needed to read the primers and build DNA.
Denaturation is the step when primers extend the new DNA strand, so lowering annealing temperature should not affect which sequence is amplified.
The result proves that PCR changes the DNA sequence of the gene itself in the original organism.
questions.explain: A mismatch at a primer-binding site can reduce amplification because primer annealing becomes less effective.
choice_explanations: Correct. PCR depends on primer-template complementarity at the flanking regions.
Ribosomes are used for translation, not DNA amplification in PCR.
This choice mixes up PCR stages. Denaturation separates DNA strands; annealing is the step where primers bind and specificity matters.
PCR amplifies DNA in a reaction tube. It does not change the DNA sequence in the original organism simply by being run.

questions.text:
After 25 PCR cycles, the amount of target DNA is much greater than after 5 cycles under otherwise identical conditions.
Which explanation best matches the outcome?
choices: PCR failed because ribosomes were not added; they are needed to read the primers and build DNA.
Denaturation is the step when primers extend the new DNA strand, so lowering annealing temperature should not affect which sequence is amplified.
The result proves that PCR changes the DNA sequence of the gene itself in the original organism.
Repeated cycles of denaturation, annealing, and extension allow exponential amplification of the target DNA sequence.
questions.explain: Repeated cycles of denaturation, annealing, and extension allow exponential amplification of the target DNA sequence.
choice_explanations: Ribosomes are used for translation, not DNA amplification in PCR.
This choice mixes up PCR stages. Denaturation separates DNA strands; annealing is the step where primers bind and specificity matters.
PCR amplifies DNA in a reaction tube. It does not change the DNA sequence in the original organism simply by being run.
Correct. PCR increases copy number dramatically over cycles when reagents remain available.

questions.text:
A PCR reaction is run for 30 cycles with both primers, nucleotides, thermostable DNA polymerase, and template DNA. A strong band appears at 450 bp. In a parallel tube missing primers, no 450-bp band appears.
Which explanation best matches the outcome?
choices: The result proves that PCR changes the DNA sequence of the gene itself in the original organism.
Primers are required to define the target region and provide starting points for DNA polymerase during PCR.
PCR failed because ribosomes were not added; they are needed to read the primers and build DNA.
Denaturation is the step when primers extend the new DNA strand, so lowering annealing temperature should not affect which sequence is amplified.
questions.explain: Primers are required to define the target region and provide starting points for DNA polymerase during PCR.

Ribosomes are used for translation, not DNA amplification in PCR.
This choice mixes up PCR stages. Denaturation separates DNA strands; annealing is the step where primers bind and specificity matters.

questions.text:
A student lowers the annealing temperature far below the recommended value. The gel shows several unexpected bands in addition to the target band.
Which explanation best matches the outcome?
choices: The low annealing temperature likely allowed primers to bind nonspecifically, producing extra PCR products.
PCR failed because ribosomes were not added; they are needed to read the primers and build DNA.
Denaturation is the step when primers extend the new DNA strand, so lowering annealing temperature should not affect which sequence is amplified.
The result proves that PCR changes the DNA sequence of the gene itself in the original organism.

questions.text:
A mutation occurs in one primer-binding site on the template. PCR yield drops sharply even though the target region itself is mostly unchanged.
Which explanation best matches the outcome?
choices: Denaturation is the step when primers extend the new DNA strand, so lowering annealing temperature should not affect which sequence is amplified.
The result proves that PCR changes the DNA sequence of the gene itself in the original organism.
A mismatch at a primer-binding site can reduce amplification because primer annealing becomes less effective.
PCR failed because ribosomes were not added; they are needed to read the primers and build DNA.

Correct. PCR depends on primer-template complementarity at the flanking regions.

questions.text:
Bacteria are grown for many generations in 15N medium, then shifted to 14N medium. After one round of DNA replication, all DNA molecules form a single band at an intermediate density.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The pattern must be caused by translation errors because ribosomes control whether DNA is heavy or light.
The results show that DNA strands are synthesized 3′→5′, which is why densities shift over generations.
Each DNA molecule contains one old heavy strand and one new light strand after one round, which supports semiconservative replication.
The data support conservative replication because one parental double helix always stays intact while an entirely new double helix is made in the first generation.
questions.explain: Each DNA molecule contains one old heavy strand and one new light strand after one round, which supports semiconservative replication.
choice_explanations: DNA density in these experiments depends on isotope-labeled nucleotides in DNA, not on ribosome activity.
Density-shift experiments test how old and new strands are distributed, not the direction of nucleotide addition.
Correct. An intermediate band after one generation is the classic expected result for semiconservative replication.
Conservative replication would predict separate heavy and light bands after the first generation, not a single intermediate band.

questions.text:
In the same isotope experiment, after two rounds of replication, DNA separates into one light band and one intermediate band.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The results show that DNA strands are synthesized 3′→5′, which is why densities shift over generations.
After two rounds, some molecules are entirely light while others still contain one original heavy strand paired with a light strand.
The data support conservative replication because one parental double helix always stays intact while an entirely new double helix is made in the first generation.
The pattern must be caused by translation errors because ribosomes control whether DNA is heavy or light.
questions.explain: After two rounds, some molecules are entirely light while others still contain one original heavy strand paired with a light strand.
choice_explanations: Density-shift experiments test how old and new strands are distributed, not the direction of nucleotide addition.
Correct. Semiconservative replication predicts both hybrid and light DNA after the second generation in light medium.
Conservative replication would predict separate heavy and light bands after the first generation, not a single intermediate band.
DNA density in these experiments depends on isotope-labeled nucleotides in DNA, not on ribosome activity.

questions.text:
A researcher labels newly synthesized DNA with a nucleotide analog only during S phase. Fluorescent signal appears at replication forks but not on RNA molecules.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The analog was incorporated into newly made DNA during replication, not into RNA during transcription.
The data support conservative replication because one parental double helix always stays intact while an entirely new double helix is made in the first generation.
The pattern must be caused by translation errors because ribosomes control whether DNA is heavy or light.
The results show that DNA strands are synthesized 3′→5′, which is why densities shift over generations.
questions.explain: The analog was incorporated into newly made DNA during replication, not into RNA during transcription.
choice_explanations: Correct. Replication-specific labeling distinguishes DNA synthesis from transcription.
Conservative replication would predict separate heavy and light bands after the first generation, not a single intermediate band.
DNA density in these experiments depends on isotope-labeled nucleotides in DNA, not on ribosome activity.
Density-shift experiments test how old and new strands are distributed, not the direction of nucleotide addition.

questions.text:
A mutant cell can unwind DNA but cannot synthesize primers. Replication forks form, but little new DNA appears.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The data support conservative replication because one parental double helix always stays intact while an entirely new double helix is made in the first generation.
The pattern must be caused by translation errors because ribosomes control whether DNA is heavy or light.
The results show that DNA strands are synthesized 3′→5′, which is why densities shift over generations.
Primers are required before DNA polymerase can extend a new strand at the fork.
questions.explain: Primers are required before DNA polymerase can extend a new strand at the fork.
choice_explanations: Conservative replication would predict separate heavy and light bands after the first generation, not a single intermediate band.
DNA density in these experiments depends on isotope-labeled nucleotides in DNA, not on ribosome activity.
Density-shift experiments test how old and new strands are distributed, not the direction of nucleotide addition.
Correct. Fork opening alone is not enough for synthesis.

questions.text:
Cells with reduced proofreading show normal total DNA amount after S phase but a higher mutation frequency in daughter cells.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The pattern must be caused by translation errors because ribosomes control whether DNA is heavy or light.
The results show that DNA strands are synthesized 3′→5′, which is why densities shift over generations.
Proofreading affects replication accuracy more than the total amount of DNA synthesized.
The data support conservative replication because one parental double helix always stays intact while an entirely new double helix is made in the first generation.
questions.explain: Proofreading affects replication accuracy more than the total amount of DNA synthesized.
choice_explanations: DNA density in these experiments depends on isotope-labeled nucleotides in DNA, not on ribosome activity.
Density-shift experiments test how old and new strands are distributed, not the direction of nucleotide addition.
Correct. A normal DNA quantity does not guarantee a low error rate.
Conservative replication would predict separate heavy and light bands after the first generation, not a single intermediate band.

questions.text:
Cells are given radiolabeled amino acids for 2 minutes and then a chase with unlabeled amino acids. In untreated cells, labeled full-length protein appears. In cells treated with a translation initiation inhibitor, labeled full-length protein is greatly reduced.
Which explanation is most consistent with the results?
choices: A stop codon causes a larger protein because the ribosome pauses there and adds more amino acids to compensate.
The inhibitor likely blocks the start of translation, so fewer ribosomes begin synthesizing the protein during the pulse.
The results are best explained by failure of DNA replication, because translation inhibitors first prevent chromosome copying before they affect proteins.
The anticodon mutation changes the promoter sequence of the gene, so transcription starts at a different place.
questions.explain: The inhibitor likely blocks the start of translation, so fewer ribosomes begin synthesizing the protein during the pulse.
choice_explanations: A stop codon ends translation; it does not extend the protein.
Correct. Reduced labeled product after an initiation inhibitor points to fewer new polypeptides entering translation.
This confuses translation with DNA replication. The experiments directly test protein synthesis machinery.
An anticodon is part of tRNA, not promoter DNA. It affects codon recognition during translation, not transcription initiation.

questions.text:
A mutation changes the anticodon of a tRNA that normally carries lysine. During translation, a different codon is now recognized by that tRNA.
Which explanation is most consistent with the results?
choices: Changing the anticodon can redirect which mRNA codon that tRNA pairs with during translation.

The results are best explained by failure of DNA replication, because translation inhibitors first prevent chromosome copying before they affect proteins.

A stop codon causes a larger protein because the ribosome pauses there and adds more amino acids to compensate.

questions.text:
Researchers compare RT-PCR products from a normal gene and a mutant gene with a damaged splice-site sequence. The mutant lane shows a larger band than the normal lane.
Which conclusion best fits the evidence?
choices: The larger mutant product likely retained an intron that is normally removed during RNA processing.
The data show that introns are translated first and then removed from the protein after synthesis.
Because the coding exons are unchanged, the protein must be identical; RNA processing cannot alter the final product.
Splicing occurs at the ribosome in the cytoplasm, so a nuclear splice defect should not matter.
questions.explain: The larger mutant product likely retained an intron that is normally removed during RNA processing.
choice_explanations: Correct. Failure to remove an intron increases mRNA length.
Introns are removed from RNA before translation; they are not translated and then removed from protein.
Changes in splicing can alter which exons are included, where stop codons appear, or how the reading frame is organized, so protein output can change greatly.
Splicing is a eukaryotic RNA-processing event associated with the nucleus, not a ribosomal function in the cytoplasm.

questions.text:
A mutation causes exon 3 to be skipped during splicing. Sequencing of mature mRNA shows exon 2 joined directly to exon 4.
Which conclusion best fits the evidence?
choices: The data show that introns are translated first and then removed from the protein after synthesis.
Because the coding exons are unchanged, the protein must be identical; RNA processing cannot alter the final product.
Splicing occurs at the ribosome in the cytoplasm, so a nuclear splice defect should not matter.
The spliceosome likely removed exon 3 along with nearby introns, producing exon skipping.
questions.explain: The spliceosome likely removed exon 3 along with nearby introns, producing exon skipping.
choice_explanations: Introns are removed from RNA before translation; they are not translated and then removed from protein.
Changes in splicing can alter which exons are included, where stop codons appear, or how the reading frame is organized, so protein output can change greatly.
Splicing is a eukaryotic RNA-processing event associated with the nucleus, not a ribosomal function in the cytoplasm.
Correct. Alternative or faulty splicing can change which exons remain in mature mRNA.

questions.text:

The data show that introns are translated first and then removed from the protein after synthesis.
questions.explain: Introns are removed before mature mRNA is exported from the nucleus.

Splicing is a eukaryotic RNA-processing event associated with the nucleus, not a ribosomal function in the cytoplasm.

questions.text:
Cells treated with a spliceosome inhibitor accumulate unprocessed RNA, and protein output from the gene drops.
Which conclusion best fits the evidence?
choices: Splicing occurs at the ribosome in the cytoplasm, so a nuclear splice defect should not matter.
Blocking splicing can reduce production of functional mature mRNA and therefore reduce translation.
The data show that introns are translated first and then removed from the protein after synthesis.
Because the coding exons are unchanged, the protein must be identical; RNA processing cannot alter the final product.
questions.explain: Blocking splicing can reduce production of functional mature mRNA and therefore reduce translation.
choice_explanations: Splicing is a eukaryotic RNA-processing event associated with the nucleus, not a ribosomal function in the cytoplasm.

Splicing occurs at the ribosome in the cytoplasm, so a nuclear splice defect should not matter.
A splice-site mutation can change the final mRNA and protein without changing the exon sequences themselves.
The data show that introns are translated first and then removed from the protein after synthesis.
questions.explain: A splice-site mutation can change the final mRNA and protein without changing the exon sequences themselves.
choice_explanations: Changes in splicing can alter which exons are included, where stop codons appear, or how the reading frame is organized, so protein output can change greatly.
Splicing is a eukaryotic RNA-processing event associated with the nucleus, not a ribosomal function in the cytoplasm.
Correct. Regulation and processing can alter proteins without altering every coding exon.
Introns are removed from RNA before translation; they are not translated and then removed from protein.

questions.text:
Researchers compare RT-PCR products from a normal gene and a mutant gene with a damaged splice-site sequence. The mutant lane shows a larger band than the normal lane.
Which conclusion best fits the evidence?
choices: Splicing occurs at the ribosome in the cytoplasm, so a nuclear splice defect should not matter.
The larger mutant product likely retained an intron that is normally removed during RNA processing.
The data show that introns are translated first and then removed from the protein after synthesis.
Because the coding exons are unchanged, the protein must be identical; RNA processing cannot alter the final product.
questions.explain: The larger mutant product likely retained an intron that is normally removed during RNA processing.
choice_explanations: Splicing is a eukaryotic RNA-processing event associated with the nucleus, not a ribosomal function in the cytoplasm.
Correct. Failure to remove an intron increases mRNA length.
Introns are removed from RNA before translation; they are not translated and then removed from protein.
Changes in splicing can alter which exons are included, where stop codons appear, or how the reading frame is organized, so protein output can change greatly.

questions.text:
A mutation causes exon 3 to be skipped during splicing. Sequencing of mature mRNA shows exon 2 joined directly to exon 4.
Which conclusion best fits the evidence?
choices: The spliceosome likely removed exon 3 along with nearby introns, producing exon skipping.
The data show that introns are translated first and then removed from the protein after synthesis.
Because the coding exons are unchanged, the protein must be identical; RNA processing cannot alter the final product.
Splicing occurs at the ribosome in the cytoplasm, so a nuclear splice defect should not matter.
questions.explain: The spliceosome likely removed exon 3 along with nearby introns, producing exon skipping.
choice_explanations: Correct. Alternative or faulty splicing can change which exons remain in mature mRNA.
Introns are removed from RNA before translation; they are not translated and then removed from protein.
Changes in splicing can alter which exons are included, where stop codons appear, or how the reading frame is organized, so protein output can change greatly.
Splicing is a eukaryotic RNA-processing event associated with the nucleus, not a ribosomal function in the cytoplasm.

questions.text:
A researcher isolates nuclear pre-mRNA and cytoplasmic mRNA from the same eukaryotic cells. The nuclear RNA contains introns, but the cytoplasmic mRNA does not.
Which conclusion best fits the evidence?
choices: The data show that introns are translated first and then removed from the protein after synthesis.
Because the coding exons are unchanged, the protein must be identical; RNA processing cannot alter the final product.
Splicing occurs at the ribosome in the cytoplasm, so a nuclear splice defect should not matter.
Introns are removed before mature mRNA is exported from the nucleus.
questions.explain: Introns are removed before mature mRNA is exported from the nucleus.
choice_explanations: Introns are removed from RNA before translation; they are not translated and then removed from protein.
Changes in splicing can alter which exons are included, where stop codons appear, or how the reading frame is organized, so protein output can change greatly.
Splicing is a eukaryotic RNA-processing event associated with the nucleus, not a ribosomal function in the cytoplasm.
Correct. This pattern is expected for normal RNA processing in eukaryotes.

questions.text:
Cells treated with a spliceosome inhibitor accumulate unprocessed RNA, and protein output from the gene drops.
Which conclusion best fits the evidence?
choices: Because the coding exons are unchanged, the protein must be identical; RNA processing cannot alter the final product.
Splicing occurs at the ribosome in the cytoplasm, so a nuclear splice defect should not matter.
Blocking splicing can reduce production of functional mature mRNA and therefore reduce translation.
The data show that introns are translated first and then removed from the protein after synthesis.

choice_explanations: Changes in splicing can alter which exons are included, where stop codons appear, or how the reading frame is organized, so protein output can change greatly.
Splicing is a eukaryotic RNA-processing event associated with the nucleus, not a ribosomal function in the cytoplasm.
Correct. Proper RNA processing is often required before efficient protein synthesis can occur.
Introns are removed from RNA before translation; they are not translated and then removed from protein.

questions.text:
A gene's coding exons are unchanged, but a mutation in an intronic splice signal produces a different protein isoform.
Which conclusion best fits the evidence?
choices: Splicing occurs at the ribosome in the cytoplasm, so a nuclear splice defect should not matter.
A splice-site mutation can change the final mRNA and protein without changing the exon sequences themselves.
The data show that introns are translated first and then removed from the protein after synthesis.

choice_explanations: Splicing is a eukaryotic RNA-processing event associated with the nucleus, not a ribosomal function in the cytoplasm.
Correct. Regulation and processing can alter proteins without altering every coding exon.

questions.text:
A stem cell line is induced to become neurons. Before induction, Gene N mRNA is 2 units and keratin mRNA is 40 units. After induction, Gene N mRNA is 85 units and keratin mRNA is 5 units.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The data show that differentiation occurred because one cell type lost all genes it no longer needed.
The results mean that the promoter sequence changed into a different protein-coding region during differentiation.
Both cell types should eventually express all genes equally once development is complete.
Differentiation changed which genes were expressed, increasing neuron-associated transcripts while reducing transcripts linked to a different cell identity.
questions.explain: Differentiation changed which genes were expressed, increasing neuron-associated transcripts while reducing transcripts linked to a different cell identity.
choice_explanations: Differentiated cells usually keep most of the same DNA; they do not need to delete all unused genes to specialize.
Promoters are regulatory DNA sequences, and differentiation is generally about gene regulation, not promoter-to-coding-sequence conversion.
Specialization depends on selective expression, so mature cell types do not all express every gene equally.
Correct. The data support selective gene expression during specialization.

questions.text:
Muscle cells and liver cells from the same organism are compared by RNA sequencing. Both contain the albumin gene and the myosin gene, but albumin RNA is high only in liver and myosin RNA is high only in muscle.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The results mean that the promoter sequence changed into a different protein-coding region during differentiation.
Both cell types should eventually express all genes equally once development is complete.
The two cell types have the same genome but express different subsets of genes.
The data show that differentiation occurred because one cell type lost all genes it no longer needed.
questions.explain: The two cell types have the same genome but express different subsets of genes.
choice_explanations: Promoters are regulatory DNA sequences, and differentiation is generally about gene regulation, not promoter-to-coding-sequence conversion.
Specialization depends on selective expression, so mature cell types do not all express every gene equally.
Correct. Cell specialization usually reflects differential gene expression, not completely different DNA sets.
Differentiated cells usually keep most of the same DNA; they do not need to delete all unused genes to specialize.

questions.text:

Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: Both cell types should eventually express all genes equally once development is complete.

The data show that differentiation occurred because one cell type lost all genes it no longer needed.
The results mean that the promoter sequence changed into a different protein-coding region during differentiation.

choice_explanations: Specialization depends on selective expression, so mature cell types do not all express every gene equally.
Correct. Homeobox genes are regulatory genes that affect development.

Promoters are regulatory DNA sequences, and differentiation is generally about gene regulation, not promoter-to-coding-sequence conversion.

questions.text:
A fluorescent reporter is placed under control of a neural-specific promoter. Only cells exposed to a neural differentiation signal glow green.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The promoter is active only in cells that adopted the neural gene-expression program.
The data show that differentiation occurred because one cell type lost all genes it no longer needed.
The results mean that the promoter sequence changed into a different protein-coding region during differentiation.
Both cell types should eventually express all genes equally once development is complete.
questions.explain: The promoter is active only in cells that adopted the neural gene-expression program.
choice_explanations: Correct. A reporter gene can reveal cell-type-specific transcription.
Differentiated cells usually keep most of the same DNA; they do not need to delete all unused genes to specialize.
Promoters are regulatory DNA sequences, and differentiation is generally about gene regulation, not promoter-to-coding-sequence conversion.
Specialization depends on selective expression, so mature cell types do not all express every gene equally.

questions.text:
During differentiation, chromatin near Gene M becomes more open in one lineage and remains condensed in another. Gene M is transcribed only in the lineage with open chromatin.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The data show that differentiation occurred because one cell type lost all genes it no longer needed.
The results mean that the promoter sequence changed into a different protein-coding region during differentiation.
Both cell types should eventually express all genes equally once development is complete.
Chromatin accessibility is associated with cell-type-specific expression of Gene M.
questions.explain: Chromatin accessibility is associated with cell-type-specific expression of Gene M.
choice_explanations: Differentiated cells usually keep most of the same DNA; they do not need to delete all unused genes to specialize.
Promoters are regulatory DNA sequences, and differentiation is generally about gene regulation, not promoter-to-coding-sequence conversion.
Specialization depends on selective expression, so mature cell types do not all express every gene equally.
Correct. Open chromatin generally supports easier access by transcription machinery.

questions.text:
Researchers use CRISPR-Cas9 to create a frameshift early in the coding region of Gene P. Cells with the edit produce almost no functional Gene P protein.
Which conclusion best matches the evidence?
choices: A promoter edit should change the amino acid sequence directly because promoters are translated before the coding region.
Cas9 works by converting mRNA into DNA, so guide RNA is only needed when editing RNA viruses.
An early frameshift likely altered downstream codons and often introduces a premature stop codon, reducing functional protein production.
The edit must have changed every gene in the genome because CRISPR cannot target specific sites.
questions.explain: An early frameshift likely altered downstream codons and often introduces a premature stop codon, reducing functional protein production.
choice_explanations: Promoters are regulatory DNA sequences, not translated parts of the protein.
This confuses Cas9 with reverse transcriptase. Cas9 cuts DNA; it does not copy RNA into DNA.
Correct. Editing near the beginning of the coding region can strongly disrupt translation of the normal protein.
CRISPR-Cas9 is designed to target specific sequences using guide-RNA complementarity; it does not automatically rewrite the whole genome.

questions.text:
A guide RNA targets the promoter of Gene Q rather than its coding region. After editing, Gene Q mRNA falls sharply, but the remaining proteins have the normal amino acid sequence.
Which conclusion best matches the evidence?
choices: Cas9 works by converting mRNA into DNA, so guide RNA is only needed when editing RNA viruses.
The edit most likely disrupted transcriptional regulation rather than protein-coding sequence.
The edit must have changed every gene in the genome because CRISPR cannot target specific sites.
A promoter edit should change the amino acid sequence directly because promoters are translated before the coding region.
questions.explain: The edit most likely disrupted transcriptional regulation rather than protein-coding sequence.
choice_explanations: This confuses Cas9 with reverse transcriptase. Cas9 cuts DNA; it does not copy RNA into DNA.
Correct. Promoter edits can reduce expression without changing the sequence of proteins made from transcripts that still occur.
CRISPR-Cas9 is designed to target specific sequences using guide-RNA complementarity; it does not automatically rewrite the whole genome.
Promoters are regulatory DNA sequences, not translated parts of the protein.

questions.text:
CRISPR is used to correct a disease-causing point mutation in a cultured cell line. After editing, the normal restriction digest pattern returns.
Which conclusion best matches the evidence?
choices: The edit must have changed every gene in the genome because CRISPR cannot target specific sites.
A promoter edit should change the amino acid sequence directly because promoters are translated before the coding region.
Cas9 works by converting mRNA into DNA, so guide RNA is only needed when editing RNA viruses.
Restoring the original DNA sequence can restore the original restriction site or banding pattern.
questions.explain: Restoring the original DNA sequence can restore the original restriction site or banding pattern.
choice_explanations: CRISPR-Cas9 is designed to target specific sequences using guide-RNA complementarity; it does not automatically rewrite the whole genome.
Promoters are regulatory DNA sequences, not translated parts of the protein.
This confuses Cas9 with reverse transcriptase. Cas9 cuts DNA; it does not copy RNA into DNA.
Correct. A point mutation can change a digest pattern if it affects a recognition sequence.

questions.text:
Two guide RNAs are used to delete a regulatory enhancer. The coding sequence of Gene S remains intact, but its mRNA level drops.
Which conclusion best matches the evidence?
choices: Cas9 works by converting mRNA into DNA, so guide RNA is only needed when editing RNA viruses.
Deleting the enhancer likely reduced transcription of Gene S even though the protein-coding exons were not changed.
The edit must have changed every gene in the genome because CRISPR cannot target specific sites.
A promoter edit should change the amino acid sequence directly because promoters are translated before the coding region.
questions.explain: Deleting the enhancer likely reduced transcription of Gene S even though the protein-coding exons were not changed.

questions.text:
Researchers use CRISPR-Cas9 to create a frameshift early in the coding region of Gene P. Cells with the edit produce almost no functional Gene P protein.
Which conclusion best matches the evidence?
choices: An early frameshift likely altered downstream codons and often introduces a premature stop codon, reducing functional protein production.
The edit must have changed every gene in the genome because CRISPR cannot target specific sites.
A promoter edit should change the amino acid sequence directly because promoters are translated before the coding region.
Cas9 works by converting mRNA into DNA, so guide RNA is only needed when editing RNA viruses.
questions.explain: An early frameshift likely altered downstream codons and often introduces a premature stop codon, reducing functional protein production.
choice_explanations: Correct. Editing near the beginning of the coding region can strongly disrupt translation of the normal protein.
CRISPR-Cas9 is designed to target specific sequences using guide-RNA complementarity; it does not automatically rewrite the whole genome.
Promoters are regulatory DNA sequences, not translated parts of the protein.
This confuses Cas9 with reverse transcriptase. Cas9 cuts DNA; it does not copy RNA into DNA.

questions.text:

Correct. Promoter edits can reduce expression without changing the sequence of proteins made from transcripts that still occur.

questions.text:
Bacteria are plated on ampicillin plates after treatment with a plasmid carrying an ampicillin-resistance gene. Colonies appear only on the plate exposed to plasmid DNA.
Which explanation is most accurate?
choices: Transformation means the bacteria changed into a different species, so selection is used to confirm speciation.
Some bacteria were transformed with the plasmid and gained antibiotic resistance.
Antibiotic resistance appears because the bacteria immediately mutate their chromosome in response to the antibiotic, not because of plasmid uptake.
GFP fluorescence proves that the plasmid DNA was translated directly by ribosomes before it was transcribed.
questions.explain: Some bacteria were transformed with the plasmid and gained antibiotic resistance.
choice_explanations: In this context, transformation means uptake of external DNA, not conversion into a different species.
Correct. Growth on antibiotic after plasmid exposure indicates successful uptake of the resistance plasmid.
This ignores the transformation setup. The experiment specifically tests whether external plasmid DNA was taken up, not whether all cells spontaneously mutated.
Genes on plasmids still follow normal gene expression logic: DNA is transcribed to mRNA, and mRNA is translated by ribosomes.

questions.text:
A plasmid contains a GFP reporter controlled by an inducible promoter. Transformed cells glow only after the inducer is added.
Which explanation is most accurate?
choices: The reporter gene is present, but expression depends on activation of the inducible promoter.
Antibiotic resistance appears because the bacteria immediately mutate their chromosome in response to the antibiotic, not because of plasmid uptake.
GFP fluorescence proves that the plasmid DNA was translated directly by ribosomes before it was transcribed.
Transformation means the bacteria changed into a different species, so selection is used to confirm speciation.
questions.explain: The reporter gene is present, but expression depends on activation of the inducible promoter.
choice_explanations: Correct. DNA uptake and gene expression are separate steps.
This ignores the transformation setup. The experiment specifically tests whether external plasmid DNA was taken up, not whether all cells spontaneously mutated.
Genes on plasmids still follow normal gene expression logic: DNA is transcribed to mRNA, and mRNA is translated by ribosomes.
In this context, transformation means uptake of external DNA, not conversion into a different species.

questions.text:
Cells exposed to plasmid DNA are plated on medium without antibiotic. Both transformed and untransformed cells grow.
Which explanation is most accurate?
choices: Antibiotic resistance appears because the bacteria immediately mutate their chromosome in response to the antibiotic, not because of plasmid uptake.
GFP fluorescence proves that the plasmid DNA was translated directly by ribosomes before it was transcribed.
Transformation means the bacteria changed into a different species, so selection is used to confirm speciation.
Nonselective medium does not distinguish transformed from untransformed cells.
questions.explain: Nonselective medium does not distinguish transformed from untransformed cells.
choice_explanations: This ignores the transformation setup. The experiment specifically tests whether external plasmid DNA was taken up, not whether all cells spontaneously mutated.
Genes on plasmids still follow normal gene expression logic: DNA is transcribed to mRNA, and mRNA is translated by ribosomes.
In this context, transformation means uptake of external DNA, not conversion into a different species.
Correct. Without selection, both populations can survive.

questions.text:
A plasmid is cut with a restriction enzyme and ligated to a foreign gene. After transformation, some colonies contain the insert and some do not.
Which explanation is most accurate?
choices: GFP fluorescence proves that the plasmid DNA was translated directly by ribosomes before it was transcribed.
Transformation means the bacteria changed into a different species, so selection is used to confirm speciation.
Ligation and transformation are not perfectly efficient, so screening colonies is needed to identify recombinant plasmids.
Antibiotic resistance appears because the bacteria immediately mutate their chromosome in response to the antibiotic, not because of plasmid uptake.
questions.explain: Ligation and transformation are not perfectly efficient, so screening colonies is needed to identify recombinant plasmids.
choice_explanations: Genes on plasmids still follow normal gene expression logic: DNA is transcribed to mRNA, and mRNA is translated by ribosomes.
In this context, transformation means uptake of external DNA, not conversion into a different species.
Correct. Not every colony will carry the desired recombinant DNA construct.
This ignores the transformation setup. The experiment specifically tests whether external plasmid DNA was taken up, not whether all cells spontaneously mutated.

questions.text:
A plasmid with a defective origin of replication enters bacteria. The cells briefly express the plasmid gene, but the plasmid is lost from descendants.
Which explanation is most accurate?
choices: Transformation means the bacteria changed into a different species, so selection is used to confirm speciation.
Without a functional origin, the plasmid is not stably replicated and inherited.
Antibiotic resistance appears because the bacteria immediately mutate their chromosome in response to the antibiotic, not because of plasmid uptake.
GFP fluorescence proves that the plasmid DNA was translated directly by ribosomes before it was transcribed.
questions.explain: Without a functional origin, the plasmid is not stably replicated and inherited.
choice_explanations: In this context, transformation means uptake of external DNA, not conversion into a different species.
Correct. Short-term expression can occur even if long-term plasmid maintenance fails.
This ignores the transformation setup. The experiment specifically tests whether external plasmid DNA was taken up, not whether all cells spontaneously mutated.

questions.explain: Without a functional origin, the plasmid is not stably replicated and inherited.
choice_explanations: Genes on plasmids still follow normal gene expression logic: DNA is transcribed to mRNA, and mRNA is translated by ribosomes.
In this context, transformation means uptake of external DNA, not conversion into a different species.
Correct. Short-term expression can occur even if long-term plasmid maintenance fails.
This ignores the transformation setup. The experiment specifically tests whether external plasmid DNA was taken up, not whether all cells spontaneously mutated.

questions.text:
Cells exposed to UV light show an increased frequency of thymine dimers and, after replication, a higher mutation rate than unexposed cells.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: UV-induced DNA damage can interfere with accurate replication, increasing mutation frequency if lesions are not repaired.
The mutations must have been caused by errors in translation, because only proteins can change phenotype.
UV exposure increases mutation rate by directly changing mRNA after transcription, so DNA repair enzymes are irrelevant.
A nonsense mutation in a tumor suppressor automatically creates an oncogene that stimulates cell division.
questions.explain: UV-induced DNA damage can interfere with accurate replication, increasing mutation frequency if lesions are not repaired.
choice_explanations: Correct. Thymine dimers distort DNA structure and can promote replication errors.
Heritable mutation rate is determined by DNA changes. Translation errors can alter proteins transiently, but they do not usually create permanent inherited mutations.
UV-induced mutagenesis is important because it damages DNA. DNA repair is highly relevant because repairing the DNA can prevent later mutations.
Loss of a tumor suppressor removes a normal brake on growth, but that is not the same thing as converting it into an oncogene.

questions.text:
A cell line lacking a DNA repair enzyme accumulates more mutations after UV exposure than a normal cell line exposed to the same dose.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The mutations must have been caused by errors in translation, because only proteins can change phenotype.
UV exposure increases mutation rate by directly changing mRNA after transcription, so DNA repair enzymes are irrelevant.
A nonsense mutation in a tumor suppressor automatically creates an oncogene that stimulates cell division.
The repair-deficient cells are less able to correct DNA damage before replication.
questions.explain: The repair-deficient cells are less able to correct DNA damage before replication.
choice_explanations: Heritable mutation rate is determined by DNA changes. Translation errors can alter proteins transiently, but they do not usually create permanent inherited mutations.
UV-induced mutagenesis is important because it damages DNA. DNA repair is highly relevant because repairing the DNA can prevent later mutations.
Loss of a tumor suppressor removes a normal brake on growth, but that is not the same thing as converting it into an oncogene.
Correct. The comparison isolates the role of repair in limiting mutation rate.

questions.text:
A microRNA complementary to Gene T mRNA is introduced into cells. Gene T mRNA falls modestly, but Gene T protein falls dramatically.
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The data prove that microRNAs increase DNA replication at the target gene, which is why less protein is made.
Because the promoter is unchanged, gene expression cannot change; only DNA mutations matter.
The microRNA must have been translated into a short repressor protein that then entered the nucleus.
The microRNA likely reduced translation and/or promoted degradation of Gene T mRNA.
questions.explain: The microRNA likely reduced translation and/or promoted degradation of Gene T mRNA.
choice_explanations: MicroRNAs regulate gene expression at the RNA level; they do not reduce protein by increasing DNA replication.
Post-transcriptional mechanisms can clearly change protein output without altering promoter sequence or DNA coding sequence.
MicroRNAs are functional RNA molecules, not RNAs that are translated into proteins in order to work.
Correct. miRNA often acts post-transcriptionally and can strongly lower protein output.

questions.text:
Researchers mutate the microRNA-binding site in the 3′ UTR of Gene U mRNA. After the mutation, the microRNA no longer reduces protein production from Gene U.
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: Because the promoter is unchanged, gene expression cannot change; only DNA mutations matter.
The microRNA must have been translated into a short repressor protein that then entered the nucleus.
The microRNA effect depended on complementary binding to that mRNA sequence.
The data prove that microRNAs increase DNA replication at the target gene, which is why less protein is made.
questions.explain: The microRNA effect depended on complementary binding to that mRNA sequence.
choice_explanations: Post-transcriptional mechanisms can clearly change protein output without altering promoter sequence or DNA coding sequence.
MicroRNAs are functional RNA molecules, not RNAs that are translated into proteins in order to work.
Correct. Changing the target site can block post-transcriptional repression.
MicroRNAs regulate gene expression at the RNA level; they do not reduce protein by increasing DNA replication.

questions.text:
A small interfering RNA targeting Gene V is added to cells. Within hours, Gene V protein level decreases while unrelated proteins remain unchanged.
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The microRNA must have been translated into a short repressor protein that then entered the nucleus.
The RNA interference treatment specifically reduced expression of the targeted gene.
The data prove that microRNAs increase DNA replication at the target gene, which is why less protein is made.
Because the promoter is unchanged, gene expression cannot change; only DNA mutations matter.
questions.explain: The RNA interference treatment specifically reduced expression of the targeted gene.
choice_explanations: MicroRNAs are functional RNA molecules, not RNAs that are translated into proteins in order to work.
Correct. Sequence-specific RNAi should affect the intended transcript more than unrelated ones.
MicroRNAs regulate gene expression at the RNA level; they do not reduce protein by increasing DNA replication.
Post-transcriptional mechanisms can clearly change protein output without altering promoter sequence or DNA coding sequence.

questions.text:
Two versions of the same gene are expressed from identical promoters, but only the transcript that contains a microRNA target site is repressed.
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The difference is best explained by post-transcriptional regulation acting on the mRNA sequence, not by promoter differences.
The data prove that microRNAs increase DNA replication at the target gene, which is why less protein is made.
Because the promoter is unchanged, gene expression cannot change; only DNA mutations matter.
The microRNA must have been translated into a short repressor protein that then entered the nucleus.
questions.explain: The difference is best explained by post-transcriptional regulation acting on the mRNA sequence, not by promoter differences.
choice_explanations: Correct. The promoters are identical, so the regulatory difference lies in the transcript sequence.
MicroRNAs regulate gene expression at the RNA level; they do not reduce protein by increasing DNA replication.
Post-transcriptional mechanisms can clearly change protein output without altering promoter sequence or DNA coding sequence.
MicroRNAs are functional RNA molecules, not RNAs that are translated into proteins in order to work.

questions.text:
A stress signal causes cells to produce more of a particular microRNA. Protein from one target gene drops even though transcription of that gene does not change.
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The data prove that microRNAs increase DNA replication at the target gene, which is why less protein is made.
Because the promoter is unchanged, gene expression cannot change; only DNA mutations matter.
The microRNA must have been translated into a short repressor protein that then entered the nucleus.
The stress response likely regulated protein output after transcription via the induced microRNA.
questions.explain: The stress response likely regulated protein output after transcription via the induced microRNA.
choice_explanations: MicroRNAs regulate gene expression at the RNA level; they do not reduce protein by increasing DNA replication.
Post-transcriptional mechanisms can clearly change protein output without altering promoter sequence or DNA coding sequence.
MicroRNAs are functional RNA molecules, not RNAs that are translated into proteins in order to work.
Correct. The unchanged transcription level points away from promoter control and toward post-transcriptional control.

A small interfering RNA targeting Gene V is added to cells. Within hours, Gene V protein level decreases while unrelated proteins remain unchanged.
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The data prove that microRNAs increase DNA replication at the target gene, which is why less protein is made.
Because the promoter is unchanged, gene expression cannot change; only DNA mutations matter.
The microRNA must have been translated into a short repressor protein that then entered the nucleus.
The RNA interference treatment specifically reduced expression of the targeted gene.
questions.explain: The RNA interference treatment specifically reduced expression of the targeted gene.
choice_explanations: MicroRNAs regulate gene expression at the RNA level; they do not reduce protein by increasing DNA replication.
Post-transcriptional mechanisms can clearly change protein output without altering promoter sequence or DNA coding sequence.
MicroRNAs are functional RNA molecules, not RNAs that are translated into proteins in order to work.
Correct. Sequence-specific RNAi should affect the intended transcript more than unrelated ones.

questions.text:
Two versions of the same gene are expressed from identical promoters, but only the transcript that contains a microRNA target site is repressed.
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: Because the promoter is unchanged, gene expression cannot change; only DNA mutations matter.
The microRNA must have been translated into a short repressor protein that then entered the nucleus.
The difference is best explained by post-transcriptional regulation acting on the mRNA sequence, not by promoter differences.
The data prove that microRNAs increase DNA replication at the target gene, which is why less protein is made.
questions.explain: The difference is best explained by post-transcriptional regulation acting on the mRNA sequence, not by promoter differences.
choice_explanations: Post-transcriptional mechanisms can clearly change protein output without altering promoter sequence or DNA coding sequence.
MicroRNAs are functional RNA molecules, not RNAs that are translated into proteins in order to work.
Correct. The promoters are identical, so the regulatory difference lies in the transcript sequence.
MicroRNAs regulate gene expression at the RNA level; they do not reduce protein by increasing DNA replication.

questions.text:
A stress signal causes cells to produce more of a particular microRNA. Protein from one target gene drops even though transcription of that gene does not change.
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The microRNA must have been translated into a short repressor protein that then entered the nucleus.
The stress response likely regulated protein output after transcription via the induced microRNA.
The data prove that microRNAs increase DNA replication at the target gene, which is why less protein is made.
Because the promoter is unchanged, gene expression cannot change; only DNA mutations matter.
questions.explain: The stress response likely regulated protein output after transcription via the induced microRNA.
choice_explanations: MicroRNAs are functional RNA molecules, not RNAs that are translated into proteins in order to work.
Correct. The unchanged transcription level points away from promoter control and toward post-transcriptional control.
MicroRNAs regulate gene expression at the RNA level; they do not reduce protein by increasing DNA replication.
Post-transcriptional mechanisms can clearly change protein output without altering promoter sequence or DNA coding sequence.

questions.text:
A microRNA complementary to Gene T mRNA is introduced into cells. Gene T mRNA falls modestly, but Gene T protein falls dramatically.
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The microRNA likely reduced translation and/or promoted degradation of Gene T mRNA.
The data prove that microRNAs increase DNA replication at the target gene, which is why less protein is made.
Because the promoter is unchanged, gene expression cannot change; only DNA mutations matter.
The microRNA must have been translated into a short repressor protein that then entered the nucleus.
questions.explain: The microRNA likely reduced translation and/or promoted degradation of Gene T mRNA.
choice_explanations: Correct. miRNA often acts post-transcriptionally and can strongly lower protein output.
MicroRNAs regulate gene expression at the RNA level; they do not reduce protein by increasing DNA replication.
Post-transcriptional mechanisms can clearly change protein output without altering promoter sequence or DNA coding sequence.
MicroRNAs are functional RNA molecules, not RNAs that are translated into proteins in order to work.

questions.text:
Researchers mutate the microRNA-binding site in the 3′ UTR of Gene U mRNA. After the mutation, the microRNA no longer reduces protein production from Gene U.
Which interpretation best fits the data?
choices: The data prove that microRNAs increase DNA replication at the target gene, which is why less protein is made.
Because the promoter is unchanged, gene expression cannot change; only DNA mutations matter.
The microRNA must have been translated into a short repressor protein that then entered the nucleus.
The microRNA effect depended on complementary binding to that mRNA sequence.
questions.explain: The microRNA effect depended on complementary binding to that mRNA sequence.
choice_explanations: MicroRNAs regulate gene expression at the RNA level; they do not reduce protein by increasing DNA replication.
Post-transcriptional mechanisms can clearly change protein output without altering promoter sequence or DNA coding sequence.
MicroRNAs are functional RNA molecules, not RNAs that are translated into proteins in order to work.
Correct. Changing the target site can block post-transcriptional repression.

questions.text:
A human insulin cDNA is inserted into a bacterial plasmid downstream of a bacterial promoter. After induction, the bacteria produce the insulin polypeptide.
Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: The data prove that bacterial and human genes use completely different genetic codes, so the protein sequence must be unrelated to the codons in the inserted gene.
Because the plasmid is present, protein should always be made at high levels regardless of promoter or inducer conditions.
The result supports the near universality of the genetic code: bacterial ribosomes can translate codons from the human gene into the same amino acid sequence.
The experiment works only because bacteria splice human introns exactly the same way human cells do.
questions.explain: The result supports the near universality of the genetic code: bacterial ribosomes can translate codons from the human gene into the same amino acid sequence.
choice_explanations: The opposite is supported. The ability of bacteria to produce a human polypeptide from the inserted coding sequence supports broad conservation of the genetic code.
A plasmid can be present without being strongly transcribed. Promoters and inducers regulate whether the recombinant gene is expressed.
Correct. The key point is that codon meaning is shared broadly enough for bacteria to make a human protein.
This is specifically wrong in the common bacterial-expression setup. Bacteria generally do not process eukaryotic introns the same way eukaryotic cells do.

questions.text:
The same human gene is inserted into bacteria with no promoter in front of it. The plasmid is present, but almost no mRNA or protein is detected.
Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: Because the plasmid is present, protein should always be made at high levels regardless of promoter or inducer conditions.
The bacteria contain the gene, but without an appropriate promoter transcription is minimal.
The experiment works only because bacteria splice human introns exactly the same way human cells do.
The data prove that bacterial and human genes use completely different genetic codes, so the protein sequence must be unrelated to the codons in the inserted gene.
questions.explain: The bacteria contain the gene, but without an appropriate promoter transcription is minimal.
choice_explanations: A plasmid can be present without being strongly transcribed. Promoters and inducers regulate whether the recombinant gene is expressed.
Correct. DNA uptake alone does not guarantee expression.
This is specifically wrong in the common bacterial-expression setup. Bacteria generally do not process eukaryotic introns the same way eukaryotic cells do.
The opposite is supported. The ability of bacteria to produce a human polypeptide from the inserted coding sequence supports broad conservation of the genetic code.

questions.text:

choices: Bacteria generally do not remove eukaryotic introns, so using genomic DNA instead of intron-free cDNA can disrupt expression.
The experiment works only because bacteria splice human introns exactly the same way human cells do.

Because the plasmid is present, protein should always be made at high levels regardless of promoter or inducer conditions.

The inducer changed gene expression, not plasmid uptake, by activating transcription from the inducible promoter.
questions.explain: The inducer changed gene expression, not plasmid uptake, by activating transcription from the inducible promoter.
choice_explanations: This is specifically wrong in the common bacterial-expression setup. Bacteria generally do not process eukaryotic introns the same way eukaryotic cells do.
The opposite is supported. The ability of bacteria to produce a human polypeptide from the inserted coding sequence supports broad conservation of the genetic code.

Correct. Presence of the DNA and expression of the DNA are separate questions.

questions.text:
Two bacterial cultures carry the same recombinant plasmid. One is grown at a condition that activates the promoter, and the other is not. Only the induced culture accumulates the recombinant protein.
Which conclusion is most justified?
choices: The data prove that bacterial and human genes use completely different genetic codes, so the protein sequence must be unrelated to the codons in the inserted gene.
Because the plasmid is present, protein should always be made at high levels regardless of promoter or inducer conditions.
Promoter activation determines transcription of the recombinant gene under these conditions.
The experiment works only because bacteria splice human introns exactly the same way human cells do.
questions.explain: Promoter activation determines transcription of the recombinant gene under these conditions.
choice_explanations: The opposite is supported. The ability of bacteria to produce a human polypeptide from the inserted coding sequence supports broad conservation of the genetic code.
A plasmid can be present without being strongly transcribed. Promoters and inducers regulate whether the recombinant gene is expressed.
